# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Universal AM Process Decomposition (The "5M Framework")

To analyze *any* AM process under KTU Module 2, decompose it into these 5 components:

1. **Material State of Feedstock** → Solid (filament, sheet, wire, powder) or Liquid (resin, slurry, melt pool)?
2. **Energy Source** → Laser, Electron Beam, UV Lamp, Extrusion Heater, Heated Roller, Plasma?
3. **Bonding Mechanism** → Photopolymerization, Sintering, Melting-Solidification, Chemical Binding, Adhesive Bonding?
4. **Layer Generation** → Deposition head movement, roller spreading, recoater blade, build platform lowering?
5. **Support Strategy** → Always needed, never needed, or process-specific (e.g., powder bed is self-supporting)?

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Layer-by-Layer:** Every AM process exploits the fact that a 3D object can be mathematically decomposed into 2D cross-sections (Fourier/Planar slicing). The energy/material is delivered to *one cross-section at a time*, and adjacent cross-sections are fused. This is the central engineering insight of AM.

---

## 2.2 Process-by-Process Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Vat Photopolymerization (VPP) — SLA & DLP

**Working Principle:** A vat of liquid photopolymer resin is selectively cured (hardened) layer by layer by ultraviolet (UV) light.

- **Stereolithography (SLA):** A single UV **laser** traces each cross-section (vector scanning).
- **Digital Light Processing (DLP):** A **projector** flashes the entire layer image at once (pixel-by-pixel curing).

**Materials:** Acrylate, Epoxy, and Vinyl Ether photopolymers; some filled resins (ceramic-filled, glass-filled).

**Process Parameters:**
- Laser power (mW) / Projector intensity (mW/cm²)
- Scan speed (mm/s)
- Layer thickness (25–100 µm)
- Hatch spacing (µm)
- Critical exposure energy (E_c, mJ/cm²) — governed by the **Jacob's Working Curve Equation** (Section 3)

**Advantages:**
- Highest accuracy (±0.05 mm) of all AM processes
- Excellent surface finish (Ra < 5 µm)
- Wide range of resins (clear, tough, flexible, biocompatible)

**Applications:** Dental crowns, jewelry patterns, investment casting patterns, hearing aids, microfluidic devices, surgical guides.

**Limitations:** Requires support structures, photopolymers are photosensitive and degrade in UV, limited mechanical/thermal properties vs. engineering plastics.

---

### 2.2.2 Material Jetting (MJT) — PolyJet, Inkjet Printing

**Working Principle:** Droplets of build material (photopolymer or wax) are jetted through hundreds of micro-nozzles (analogous to a 2D inkjet printer head) and instantly cured by UV light. Multiple jetting heads allow **multi-material / multi-color** parts in a single build.

**Materials:** Photopolymer resins, wax, biocompatible resins, transparent resins, elastomers.

**Process Parameters:** Drop volume (1–80 picolitres), jetting frequency, UV exposure, build orientation, support material (gelatin-like or wax).

**Advantages:**
- Multi-material and full-color capability (CMYK-like)
- Very high resolution (X/Y resolution ~14–42 µm)
- Smooth surface (Ra ~ 1–5 µm)

**Applications:** Realistic anatomical models, prototypes requiring soft-hard material gradients, investment casting wax patterns, optics.

**Limitations:** Expensive materials, limited material properties, brittle photopolymers.

---

### 2.2.3 Binder Jetting (BJT)

**Working Principle:** A liquid bonding agent (binder) is selectively deposited from a print head onto a powder bed. The binder reacts with the powder particles and glues them together, layer by layer. After printing, the "green part" is **depowdered** and often **infiltrated** with a secondary material (epoxy, bronze) to improve density and strength.

**Materials:** Metal powders (stainless steel, bronze, sand), ceramics, sand (for foundry molds), polymers (PMMA, polystyrene).

**Process Parameters:** Binder saturation (%), layer thickness (50–200 µm), powder size distribution, binder drop volume, drying time.

**Advantages:**
- No support structures needed (powder bed is self-supporting)
- Large build volumes possible
- Full-color capability (CMYK inks in sand printing)
- Relatively fast and low-cost

**Applications:** Sand casting molds, large metal parts (after infiltration), architectural models, full-color prototypes, large tooling.

**Limitations:** Porous parts (typical density 50–70 %), require post-processing infiltration/sintering, limited accuracy vs. PBF.

---

### 2.2.4 Material Extrusion (MEX) — FDM / FFF

**Working Principle:** A thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated nozzle, melted, and extruded layer by layer onto a build platform. The extruded bead solidifies upon cooling and bonds to the previous layer.

**Materials:** ABS, PLA, PETG, TPU (flexible), Nylon, PC, PEEK, ULTEM, composites (carbon-fiber filled).

**Process Parameters:** Nozzle temperature (180–400 °C), bed temperature (25–110 °C), layer height (50–300 µm), print speed (mm/s), infill density (%), infill pattern, nozzle diameter (0.2–1.0 mm).

**Advantages:**
- Low cost, widely accessible
- Simple process, broad material range
- Minimal post-processing

**Applications:** Functional prototypes, jigs & fixtures, end-use parts, medical models, educational models, low-volume custom parts.

**Limitations:** Anisotropic mechanical properties, visible stair-stepping, limited accuracy vs. VPP/MJT.

---

### 2.2.5 Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) — SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM

**Working Principle:** A thin layer of powder is spread by a recoater, and an energy source selectively fuses the powder particles.
- **SLS (Selective Laser Sintering):** Laser sinters polymer powder (below melting point, diffusion bonding).
- **SLM / DMLS (Selective Laser Melting / Direct Metal Laser Sintering):** Laser fully melts metal powder.
- **EBM (Electron Beam Melting):** Electron beam melts metal powder in a vacuum.

**Materials:** Polyamide (PA12, PA11), TPU, PEEK, PPS; Stainless steel 316L, Ti6Al4V, AlSi10Mg, Inconel 718/625, CoCrMo, copper.

**Process Parameters:** Laser/EB power (W), scan speed (mm/s), hatch spacing (µm), layer thickness (20–80 µm), chamber atmosphere (N₂ for laser, vacuum for EBM), preheat temperature.

**Advantages:**
- High mechanical strength (SLM parts are near-fully dense, 95–99.9 %)
- No support structures needed for SLS (powder supports itself)
- Complex lattice structures, internal channels possible
- Wide range of engineering metals & polymers

**Applications:** Aerospace brackets & turbine blades, medical implants (cranial, spinal, dental), automotive functional prototypes, conformal cooling inserts in injection molds.

**Limitations:** Expensive machines & materials, post-processing required (support removal, HIP, machining), thermal residual stresses, powder handling hazards (metal powders are reactive).

---

### 2.2.6 Sheet Lamination (SHL) — LOM & UAM

**Working Principle:** Sheets of material are bonded together and cut to shape layer by layer.
- **LOM (Laminated Object Manufacturing):** Paper/composite sheets are bonded with heated rollers, then laser-cut.
- **UAM (Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing):** Metal tapes are bonded by ultrasonic welding and then CNC-machined between layers.

**Materials:** Paper, plastic films, metal foils (UAM: aluminum, copper, titanium, stainless steel).

**Process Parameters:** Roller temperature, feed rate, laser power (for cutting), ultrasonic amplitude & pressure (UAM).

**Advantages:**
- Low cost (LOM uses paper)
- High build speed for large objects
- UAM allows dissimilar metal composites (e.g., Al-Cu)

**Applications:** Sand casting patterns (LOM), foundry tooling, architectural models, metal matrix composites (UAM), embedded sensors.

**Limitations:** Limited accuracy, material waste (LOM), slow UAM.

---

### 2.2.7 Directed Energy Deposition (DED) — LMD, EBAM, WAAM

**Working Principle:** Focused thermal energy (laser, electron beam, plasma arc) is used to melt material as it is being deposited (via powder injection or wire feed). Typically used with 5+ axis robotic systems for large parts and repairs.

**Materials:** Metal powders (CoCr, Inconel, stainless steel), metal wires (Ti, Al, steel).

**Process Parameters:** Laser/EB/plasma power, feed rate, traverse speed, powder flow rate, layer height, inert gas flow.

**Advantages:**
- Very large build volumes (meters)
- Multi-axis deposition (no support in many orientations)
- Excellent for **repair and cladding** of existing parts
- Wire DED is highly material-efficient

**Applications:** Aerospace large structural parts, turbine blade repair, marine propellers, oil & gas components, large dies & molds.

**Limitations:** Lower accuracy, rough surface finish, requires machining post-processing.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{d}$ (Jacob's Cure Depth) | Depth of photopolymer cured per UV exposure | mm |
| $D_p = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)$ — **Jacob's Working Curve** | Cure depth vs. exposure energy | D_p = penetration depth (mm); E_c = critical exposure (mJ/cm²) |
| $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$ | Standard SLA cure depth form | Used in KTU numerical problems |
| $E_0 = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ | Exposure dose at a point | P = laser power; v = scan speed; h = hatch spacing; t = layer thickness |
| $V_{build} = A \cdot h_{layer}$ | Volume deposited per layer | A = cross-section area; h = layer height |
| $t_{build} = \dfrac{N_{layers}}{f}$ | Build time for n layers | N = number of layers; f = layer frequency |
| $N_{layers} = \dfrac{H_{part}}{h_{layer}}$ | Total number of layers | H = part height |
| $\rho_{part} = \dfrac{\rho_{powder}}{\eta}$ | Part density after PBF | η = packing/relative density (typically 0.95–0.999) |
| $R_a \approx 0.5 \cdot h_{layer}$ | Approx. surface roughness vs. layer thickness | Empirical for FDM/SLA |
| $\% \text{Infill} = \dfrac{V_{filled}}{V_{total}} \times 100$ | FDM infill fraction | 0–100 % |
| $S_{support} = k \cdot A_{overhang}$ | Support material area required | k = safety factor; A = overhang area |
| $V_{shrink} = V_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha \cdot \Delta T)$ | Thermal shrinkage allowance | α = CTE; ΔT = temperature drop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Syntax:** All absolute-value and "such that" bars use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table — e.g., $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$.

---

## 2.4 The "Which Process to Choose?" Decision Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "select an AM process for X," always justify using this 4-criterion framework: **Material, Accuracy, Size, Application volume.**

```
METAL PART?  ──Yes──>  SLM/DMLS (small, precise)  OR  DED (large, repair)
   │
   └──No──>  POLYMER PART?
                 │
                 ├──High accuracy? ──> SLA/DLP
                 ├──Multi-material? ──> PolyJet (MJT)
                 ├──Strong functional? ──> SLS (PA12)
                 ├──Low cost / hobby? ──> FDM
                 └──Large / full-color? ──> Binder Jetting
```

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Jacob's Working Curve (Foundation of SLA)

The single most important equation in Vat Photopolymerization. It relates the **depth of cure** $C_d$ to the **exposure energy** $E_0$ at the surface.

### Assumptions
1. Beer-Lambert law governs light absorption in the photopolymer: $I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha z}$
2. Photopolymerization occurs only where the local intensity exceeds the critical intensity $I_c$
3. $E_0$ is the energy delivered per unit area at the resin surface

### Step 1 — Set up the Beer-Lambert Absorption

Light intensity at depth $z$ inside the resin follows:

$$
I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha z}
$$

**Explanation:** As UV light penetrates deeper, the resin absorbs energy and the intensity decays exponentially. The Beer-Lambert constant $\alpha$ characterizes the resin's optical opacity.

### Step 2 — Define the Critical Depth of Cure

Cure begins when the local intensity equals the critical intensity $I_c$ required to start the photoinitiator reaction:

$$
I(z) = I_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha z} = I_c
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Cure Depth $C_d$

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$
\ln(I_0) - \alpha z = \ln(I_c)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
z = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I_c}\right)
$$

The depth of cure $C_d$ is exactly this $z$:

$$
C_d = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I_c}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Energy Notation

In practice, intensity is replaced by exposure energy $E$ and the inverse-absorption term is the **penetration depth** $D_p$:

$$
D_p = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}
$$

Critical exposure $E_c$ is defined analogously. So we get the **Jacob's Working Curve**:

$$
C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)
$$

**Where:**
- $C_d$ = depth of cure (mm)
- $D_p$ = penetration depth of light into resin (mm) — resin property
- $E_0$ = exposure energy at the surface (mJ/cm²)
- $E_c$ = critical exposure energy (mJ/cm²) — resin property

### Step 5 — Sample Numerical (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Given:** A resin has $D_p = 0.15$ mm and $E_c = 9$ mJ/cm². The SLA machine delivers $E_0 = 90$ mJ/cm² at the surface. Find the cure depth.

$$
C_d = 0.15 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{90}{9}\right)
$$

$$
C_d = 0.15 \cdot \ln(10)
$$

$$
C_d = 0.15 \cdot 2.3026
$$

$$
\boxed{C_d \approx 0.345 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Interpretation:** With a 10× over-exposure, the cure depth is 0.345 mm. To set a layer thickness of 0.1 mm, we need a smaller over-exposure so adjacent layers are properly bonded but not over-cured.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Build Time for an AM Part (FDM/SLA/SLS)

For any layer-by-layer process:

### Step 1 — Number of Layers

$$
N_{layers} = \dfrac{H_{part}}{h_{layer}}
$$

### Step 2 — Build Time

$$
t_{build} = N_{layers} \cdot t_{layer} = \dfrac{H_{part}}{h_{layer}} \cdot t_{layer}
$$

### Step 3 — For FDM (Area-based)

For FDM, $t_{layer} \approx \dfrac{A_{layer}}{v \cdot w}$ where $v$ is print speed and $w$ is extrusion width.

$$
t_{build} = \dfrac{H_{part}}{h_{layer}} \cdot \dfrac{A_{layer}}{v \cdot w}
$$

**Interpretation:** Build time grows **linearly with part height** and **inversely with speed/width**. This is why a 100 mm tall FDM part takes much longer than a 100 mm wide flat one.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q1. List any three AM process categories as per ASTM/ISO 52900, with one example process each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Vat Photopolymerization** — Example: Stereolithography (SLA). Liquid photopolymer resin is cured by a UV laser layer by layer.
2. **Powder Bed Fusion** — Example: Selective Laser Sintering (SLS). A laser fuses polymer powder particles in a bed.
3. **Material Extrusion** — Example: Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM). Thermoplastic filament is melted and extruded through a nozzle.

(Any 3 of the 7 categories with valid examples — 1 mark for category name, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for brief principle.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q2. What is Jacob's working curve? State the equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Jacob's working curve describes the relationship between the **depth of cure** ($C_d$) in a photopolymer resin and the **exposure energy** ($E_0$) delivered to the surface. It is derived from the Beer-Lambert law of light absorption.

$$
C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)
$$

where $D_p$ is the penetration depth (a resin property) and $E_c$ is the critical exposure energy needed to start polymerization (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for variable explanation).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Module 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — *FDM + Material Extrusion Focus*

**a)** Explain the working principle of Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) with a neat block diagram. List **five process parameters** that affect part quality. (7 marks)

**b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of FDM. Compare FDM with SLA in terms of accuracy, surface finish, and material range. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle of FDM (5 marks):**

FDM is a Material Extrusion process in which a continuous thermoplastic filament is fed from a spool into a heated liquefier/nozzle (1 mark). The nozzle is heated above the polymer's melting temperature (e.g., 200 °C for PLA, 240 °C for ABS) (1 mark). The molten polymer is extruded through the nozzle and deposited as a thin bead (typically 0.4 mm wide) onto a build platform (1 mark). The bead solidifies upon cooling and bonds to the previously deposited layer. The nozzle traverses in the X-Y plane, controlled by a gantry, to trace each cross-section (1 mark). After one layer, the build platform drops by one layer height (50–300 µm) and the next layer is deposited. This repeats until the part is complete (1 mark).

**Five Process Parameters (2 marks):**
1. Nozzle temperature (°C)
2. Build platform / bed temperature (°C)
3. Layer thickness / height (µm)
4. Print / traverse speed (mm/s)
5. Infill density (%) and infill pattern

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Advantages of FDM (2 marks):**
- Low capital and material cost — most accessible AM process
- Wide range of thermoplastic materials (ABS, PLA, PETG, Nylon, TPU, PEEK)
- Simple operation and minimal post-processing
- Good for functional prototypes and end-use parts
- Multiple materials and colors possible

**Limitations of FDM (2 marks):**
- Anisotropic mechanical properties (weaker along Z due to inter-layer bonding)
- Visible stair-stepping on curved surfaces
- Limited accuracy (±0.2 mm typical) compared to SLA/SLM
- Longer build times for large parts

**Applications of FDM (1 mark):**
Jigs and fixtures, functional prototypes, low-volume end-use parts, educational models, hobbyist prints, medical anatomical models.

**Comparison with SLA (2 marks):**

| Property | FDM | SLA |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±0.2 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| Surface Finish | Ra 10–25 µm (visible ridges) | Ra 1–5 µm (smooth, glossy) |
| Material Range | Thermoplastics only | Photopolymers only |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Strength | Anisotropic, depends on polymer | Brittle, low heat resistance |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *PBF / SLM Focus*

**a)** Describe the **working principle of Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** with a schematic. List the **process parameters** that govern the melt pool stability and part density. (7 marks)

**b)** A Ti6Al4V hip implant is to be manufactured by SLM. Discuss **material requirements, advantages, post-processing steps, and applications** of SLM in the medical and aerospace industries. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle of SLM (4 marks):**

Selective Laser Melting (also called DMLS — Direct Metal Laser Sintering) is a Powder Bed Fusion process for metals (1 mark). A thin layer of metal powder (20–80 µm) is spread across a build platform by a recoater blade or roller (1 mark). A high-power fiber laser (200–500 W, typically 1070 nm wavelength) is steered by galvanometer mirrors and focused onto the powder bed (1 mark). The laser fully melts the metal powder particles in a localized melt pool. The melt pool rapidly solidifies, fusing the particles into a dense solid layer (1 mark). The build platform drops by one layer thickness, fresh powder is spread, and the next layer is melted. This repeats to build the full 3D part in an inert atmosphere (N₂ or Ar) to prevent oxidation.

**Process Parameters Governing Melt Pool & Density (3 marks):**
1. **Laser Power (W)** — Higher power = deeper melt, but risk of keyhole porosity
2. **Scan Speed (mm/s)** — Slower speed = more energy input, denser parts
3. **Hatch Spacing (µm)** — Overlap between adjacent scan tracks
4. **Layer Thickness (µm)** — Thinner layers improve surface finish and density
5. **Powder morphology** — Spherical particles with size 15–45 µm improve flow and packing
6. **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** — given by $VED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ in J/mm³

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Material Requirements for Ti6Al4V (1.5 marks):**
- Spherical powder morphology for flowability
- Particle size 15–45 µm
- Low oxygen/nitrogen content to prevent embrittlement
- Atomized in inert gas (typically plasma or gas atomization)
- Biocompatible per ASTM F136 standard

**Post-Processing Steps (2 marks):**
1. **Build plate removal** — Wire EDM or band saw
2. **Support removal** — Machining or manual grinding
3. **Heat treatment / Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** — eliminates internal porosity
4. **Surface finishing** — Bead blasting, polishing, machining
5. **Sterilization** for medical implants (autoclave or gamma irradiation)
6. **Inspection** — CT scanning for internal defects

**Medical Applications (1.5 marks):**
- Patient-specific cranial implants, spinal cages
- Dental crowns, bridges, and implants
- Custom orthopedic implants (hip, knee, shoulder)
- Surgical guides and instruments
- Porous scaffolds for bone ingrowth

**Aerospace Applications (1 mark):**
- Topology-optimized brackets (BUCCANEER-style 50% weight reduction)
- Turbine blade and combustor components
- Conformal cooling channels in rocket engine injectors
- Lattice structures for thermal management

**Advantages of SLM (1 mark):**
- Near-full density (95–99.9 %)
- Complex internal geometries (lattices, channels)
- Patient-specific customization
- Part consolidation (multiple parts → one)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Module 2**
> 
> 1. **Confusing SLS vs. SLM** — SLS *sinters* polymer powder below melting point (no full melt); SLM *melts* metal powder fully. Many students write "SLS for metals" — this is wrong in classical ASTM terminology.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Jacob's working curve** — For any SLA numerical, the KTU examiner expects the equation $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0/E_c)$. Marks are lost for skipping the formula and jumping to a final number.
> 
> 3. **Stating "FDM has no support"** — Wrong. FDM always needs support for overhangs >45°. Only SLS/PBF powder processes are self-supporting.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up the 7 categories** — Vat Photopolymerization, Material Jetting, and Binder Jetting are three DIFFERENT categories — students often group them.
> 
> 5. **No units in numerical answers** — A numerical answer without mJ/cm², µm, or mm loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark questions** — Part (a) of 7-mark questions almost always requires a diagram. If you describe the principle without a block diagram, you lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — AM Processes: One-Page Revision Cheat Sheet**

### 🔑 The 7 AM Categories (ASTM F2792)
- **V**at Photopolymerization (SLA, DLP) → **Light + Resin**
- **M**aterial Jetting (PolyJet) → **Droplets + UV**
- **B**inder Jetting → **Powder + Liquid glue**
- **M**aterial Extrusion (FDM) → **Filament + Heat**
- **P**owder Bed Fusion (SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM) → **Powder + Laser/EB**
- **S**heet Lamination (LOM, UAM) → **Sheets + Adhesive/Weld**
- **D**irected Energy Deposition (LMD, EBAM, WAAM) → **Powder/Wire + Laser/Arc**

### 🔑 Key Equations
- Jacob's Cure Depth: $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0 / E_c)$
- Volumetric Energy Density: $VED = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$
- Number of layers: $N = H_{part} / h_{layer}$
- Surface roughness: $R_a \approx 0.5 \cdot h_{layer}$

### 🔑 Process-Selection Mnemonic
- **Smoothest** = SLA / DLP
- **Strongest metal** = SLM / DMLS
- **Fastest large parts** = Binder Jetting
- **Cheapest** = FDM
- **Largest** = DED
- **Multi-material** = PolyJet (MJT)
- **No supports** = SLS, BJT, LOM, UAM

### 🔑 Must-remember Material Facts
- **SLA resins** are photosensitive — degrade in sunlight
- **SLM metals** require inert chamber (N₂ / Ar) — Ti6Al4V, 316L SS, AlSi10Mg, Inconel 718
- **EBM** runs in **vacuum** with **electron beam** (not laser)
- **FDM filaments** are hygroscopic — must be dried before printing
- **PA12 Nylon** is the workhorse SLS material
- **Binder Jetting** parts are **porous** — require infiltration

### 🔑 Common Exam Facts
- ASTM F2792 = the standard that defines 7 AM categories
- ISO/ASTM 52900 = the updated international version
- STL format = Standard Tessellation Language (slicing into triangles)
- "Green part" = part as-built in binder jetting (before infiltration)
- "Melt pool" = localized molten zone in PBF / DED processes
- Support structures are sacrificial — used to anchor overhangs

### 🔑 Process Parameter Categories (3 Universal Groups)
1. **Energy input** — power, scan speed, exposure time
2. **Layer / Geometry** — layer thickness, hatch spacing, infill
3. **Material** — particle size, morphology, temperature

### 🔑 Top 3 KTU Frequently Asked Topics
1. Comparison of SLA vs. SLS vs. FDM (Table-format answer — practice this!)
2. Jacob's Working Curve derivation + numerical
3. Selection of AM process for a given application (use the decision logic in Section 2.4)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stereo Lithography (SLA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET616 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stereo Lithography (SLA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Stereo Lithography (SLA) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Stereo Lithography (SLA)** is a **vat photopolymerization** based additive manufacturing process, patented by **Chuck Hull in 1986** (3D Systems Corp.), in which a focused **ultra-violet (UV) laser beam** selectively cures (hardens) a liquid photopolymer resin point-by-point and layer-by-layer to fabricate a solid three-dimensional object directly from a CAD model.

---

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "Building a Cake with a Surgical Laser"

Imagine you have a **shallow tray of honey** sitting on a table. Now, suppose a tiny invisible surgical beam sweeps across the surface of the honey, and wherever the beam touches, the honey **instantly hardens into solid candy**. After the beam finishes drawing one flat outline (say, the shape of a flower), the tray lowers by exactly the thickness of a sheet of paper. A new thin film of honey flows over the previously hardened candy, and the beam draws the next slice of the flower — slightly larger, slightly different. Layer by layer, a 3D candy flower is built from the bottom up.

**SLA works exactly this way** — the *honey* is a **photopolymer liquid resin**, and the *invisible surgical beam* is a **UV laser**. Wherever the laser hits, the resin undergoes **photo-polymerization** (a chemical reaction triggered by light), instantly changing from liquid to solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam**
> SLA is the **first-ever commercialized AM process** (1986) and is the **pioneer technology** of the entire 3D-printing industry. It belongs to the **"Vat Photopolymerization"** family as per **ASTM/ISO 52900** classification of AM processes.

---

## 1.2 Classification & Position in AM Family

| Aspect | SLA Classification |
|---|---|
| ISO/ASTM 52900 Category | **Vat Photopolymerization (VPP)** |
| Energy Source | **Photonic (UV Light / Laser)** |
| State of Starting Material | **Liquid (Photopolymer Resin)** |
| Layer-Formation Method | **Point-by-point Laser Curing** |
| Accuracy Class | **High (±0.1 mm typical)** |
| Surface Finish | **Excellent (Ra 0.5–2 μm)** |

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary You Must Memorize

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vocabulary Block (3-Mark Favorite)**
> - **Photopolymer** → A liquid resin containing **monomers + photoinitiators** that hardens when exposed to specific wavelength of light.
> - **Photoinitiator** → A special molecule that **absorbs UV photons** and generates **free radicals** that trigger the polymerization chain reaction.
> - **Cure Depth (Cd)** → The vertical thickness of resin that is solidified in a single laser pass.
> - **Cure Width (Cw)** → The lateral width of the solidified line on a single scan.
> - **Resin Vat / Tank** → The container holding the liquid photopolymer.
> - **Build Platform** → The substrate on which the part is built (dipped into the vat).
> - **Galvanometer Mirrors (Galvos)** → High-speed rotating mirrors that steer the laser beam across the resin surface.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Sub-Systems of an SLA Machine

A typical SLA machine is composed of the following sub-systems. KTU examiners frequently ask to "list the components of an SLA machine" for 3–5 marks.

1. **Resin Vat (Tank)** — Holds the liquid photopolymer; bottom is a transparent window (glass/FEP film) for laser entry (in *bottom-up* configuration).
2. **Build Platform** — Vertical elevator that moves the part; precision Z-axis stage (typical step **25–100 μm**).
3. **UV Laser Source** — Either a **He-Cd laser (λ ≈ 325 nm)** or a **UV solid-state laser (λ ≈ 355 nm, Nd:YVO₄ tripled)**.
4. **Beam-Steering Optics** — Two **X & Y galvanometer mirrors** that rapidly deflect the laser beam across the vat surface.
5. **F-Theta Lens** — Focuses the laser to a small spot (typical diameter **75–250 μm**).
6. **Recoater / Wiper Blade** — Spreads a fresh, uniform layer of liquid resin after each cured layer.
7. **Control Computer & Software** — Slices the 3D CAD model, generates scan paths, and controls all hardware synchronously.

---

## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Configurations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic — 7 Mark Question**
> Differentiate between *Free Surface (Bat)* and *Constrained Surface (Scroll)* configurations of SLA.

| Feature | Free Surface (Bat Configuration) | Constrained Surface (Scroll Configuration) |
|---|---|---|
| Platform Position | **Above** the resin surface | **Below** the resin surface (inside vat) |
| Laser Entry | Direct from above the vat | From **below**, through transparent window |
| Resin Level | High fill, open surface | Low fill, resin sandwiched between platform & window |
| Oxygen Inhibition | **High** (oxygen at surface inhibits cure) | **Low** (oxygen excluded) |
| Part Separation Force | Lower | Higher (vacuum + adhesion to window) |
| Accuracy | Lower | **Higher** (no peel forces) |
| Modern Usage | Older / industrial | **Most modern desktop SLA** (Formlabs-style) |

---

## 2.3 The Step-by-Step Process Flow of SLA

The SLA process executes the following sequential loop **once per layer**:

1. **CAD Slicing** — The 3D model is sliced into horizontal cross-sections of thickness **Δz (25–100 μm)** using a slicer software.
2. **Platform Positioning** — The build platform is positioned at the correct Z-height for the current layer.
3. **Resin Recoating** — A recoater blade sweeps a fresh, uniform film of liquid resin across the working surface.
4. **Laser Scanning** — The UV laser, steered by the galvano-mirrors, traces the 2D cross-section, **polymerizing** the resin along the scan path.
5. **Layer Curing** — The freshly polymerized layer bonds to the previous layer (inter-layer adhesion).
6. **Z-Increment** — The build platform moves by **Δz** (downward in *free-surface*, upward in *constrained-surface*).
7. **Repeat** — Steps 3–6 are repeated until the part is complete.
8. **Post-Processing** — The part is removed, drained, washed (usually in **IPA / isopropyl alcohol**), and **post-cured** in a UV chamber.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Stereolithography" is the word?**
> The prefix *stereo* comes from Greek meaning **"solid / three-dimensional"**, and *lithography* means **"writing with light"** (originally used in 2D printing). Thus, SLA literally = **"writing a 3D solid with light"** — coined by Chuck Hull.

---

## 2.4 The Photochemistry of Curing (Simplified)

When a UV photon hits a **photoinitiator** molecule, the molecule splits into **free radicals**. These radicals attack the **C=C double bonds** of the monomer chains (e.g., acrylates or epoxides), opening them and creating new bonds — this is **chain-growth polymerization**. The reaction **propagates rapidly**, turning liquid monomers into a cross-linked solid polymer network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** Pure resin contains **monomers + oligomers + photoinitiators (0.5–3% by weight) + UV absorbers + additives**. The exact recipe is proprietary to each resin manufacturer.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize These 4 Equations — They Appear Almost Every Year**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $$\text{Cure Depth} \quad C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$ | **Beer–Lambert law** for laser penetration into resin | $C_d$ in μm, $D_p$ in μm |
| **2** | $$\text{Energy Density} \quad E_0 = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h_s \cdot t}$$ | Laser energy delivered per unit volume of resin | J/mm² or mJ/cm² |
| **3** | $$\text{Scan Speed} \quad v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot f \cdot \theta_{\max}}{90}$$ | Linear speed of a galvo mirror (f = frequency, θ = max angle) | mm/s |
| **4** | $$\text{Cure Width} \quad C_w = D_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \cdot E_0}{2 \cdot H \cdot C_d}}$$ | Lateral width of polymerized track | μm |

**Where the symbols mean:**

- $C_d$ = **Cure depth** (μm) — the depth of resin solidified by one laser pass.
- $D_p$ = **Penetration depth** of the laser into the resin (μm) — a material property.
- $E_0$ = **Incident energy density** (mJ/cm²) at the resin surface.
- $E_c$ = **Critical energy density** (mJ/cm²) — minimum energy required to start polymerization; a material property of the resin.
- $P$ = **Laser power** (mW).
- $v$ = **Scan speed** (mm/s).
- $h_s$ = **Hatch spacing** (mm) — the lateral distance between adjacent scan lines.
- $t$ = **Layer thickness** (mm) — set by the slicer.
- $H$ = **Hildebrand parameter** / total energy per unit length for a given resin.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations**
> - Equation 1 is used to **select the correct exposure dose** so that a layer cures through its full thickness but does not over-cure adjacent regions (z-resolution).
> - Equation 2 lets you trade off between **laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, and layer thickness** — these are the four "knobs" a process engineer tunes to optimize SLA for speed vs. accuracy.
> - Equation 4 predicts the **minimum feature width** that can be printed — critical for micro-SLA and dental applications.

---

## 2.6 Key Process Parameters & Their Effect

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on Part | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Laser Power (P)** | More overcure, thicker $C_d$ | Faster build, but lower accuracy |
| **Scan Speed (v)** | Less energy delivered, thinner $C_d$ | Sharper features, but slower build |
| **Hatch Spacing ($h_s$)** | Wider scan lines, possible voids | Faster build, but weaker parts |
| **Layer Thickness (t)** | Thicker layers, stair-stepping visible | Faster build, but rough sidewalls |
| **Wavelength (λ)** | Different resin absorption | Match to photoinitiator |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Applications of SLA

- **Jewelry industry** — Investment-casting patterns for lost-wax casting (highest-volume SLA application).
- **Dental & medical** — Crowns, bridges, surgical guides, hearing-aid shells.
- **Prototyping** — Concept models, ergonomic test parts, design verification.
- **Microfluidics & MEMS** — High-resolution features (<100 μm) for lab-on-a-chip.
- **Optical components** — Lenses, light pipes using clear resins.
- **Investment casting patterns** — Master patterns for jewelry and turbine blades.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cure-Depth Equation (Beer–Lambert Law Applied to Photopolymerization)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters:**
> The cure-depth equation is the **fundamental governing equation of SLA** and is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU board exams. The model was first proposed by **Jacobs (1992)** and is universally accepted.

### 3.1.1 Setup of the Physics

Consider a UV laser beam of intensity $I$ traveling in the **+z direction** into a liquid photopolymer resin. The resin contains photoinitiators that absorb photons as the beam penetrates. The intensity therefore **decays exponentially** with depth — this is the classic **Beer–Lambert Law**.

Let:
- $I(z)$ = laser intensity at depth $z$ below the resin surface (mW/cm²).
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient of the resin (cm⁻¹).
- $z$ = depth inside the resin (μm).

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Beer–Lambert Decay of Intensity

By Beer–Lambert's law, the intensity at depth $z$ is:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot z}$$

where $I_0$ is the **incident intensity** at the resin surface (z = 0).

This equation simply states: *the deeper the laser goes, the more it gets absorbed — so intensity drops exponentially.*

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Energy Density Deposited at Depth $z$

The energy density $E(z)$ deposited at depth $z$ during a single laser pass of scan speed $v$ over a line of length $L$ is proportional to the local intensity $I(z)$ and inversely proportional to the scan speed.

$$E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot z}$$

where $E_0$ is the **incident energy density** at the surface ($z = 0$).

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Critical Energy Threshold

Polymerization only occurs if the local energy density exceeds a **critical value $E_c$**, which is a property of the specific photopolymer (depends on photoinitiator concentration, monomer reactivity, etc.).

The **cure depth $C_d$** is defined as the depth $z$ at which the energy density just equals the critical energy:

$$E(C_d) = E_c$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Solve for $C_d$

Substituting $z = C_d$ in the Beer–Lambert equation:

$$E_c = E_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot C_d}$$

Divide both sides by $E_0$:

$$\dfrac{E_c}{E_0} = e^{-\alpha \cdot C_d}$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_c}{E_0}\right) = -\alpha \cdot C_d$$

Multiply both sides by $-1$:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right) = \alpha \cdot C_d$$

Solve for $C_d$:

$$C_d = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Define Penetration Depth $D_p$

The **penetration depth $D_p$** is defined as the reciprocal of the absorption coefficient:

$$D_p = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Final Compact Form

Substituting $D_p$:

$$\boxed{\,C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)\,}$$

This is the **Jacobs Cure-Depth Equation** — the foundational equation of the SLA process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Exam**
> - $C_d \propto \ln(E_0)$ → cure depth grows **logarithmically** with exposure.
> - To get **larger $C_d$**, increase $E_0$ (raise $P$, lower $v$, reduce $h_s$, reduce $t$).
> - For valid curing, we **must have $E_0 > E_c$**; otherwise the log is undefined.
> - The **slope** of $C_d$ vs. $\ln(E_0)$ gives the penetration depth $D_p$, used as a calibration test for new resins.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Exposure Energy Density Formula

Starting from first principles, the energy density delivered per unit area of resin is:

$$E_0 = \dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Area}} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta t}{h_s \cdot \Delta L}$$

where $P$ is laser power, $\Delta t$ is dwell time per spot, and $\Delta L$ is the distance the beam moves.

Since scan speed $v = \Delta L / \Delta t$:

$$E_0 = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h_s}$$

For a layer of thickness $t$, the energy per unit **volume** (volumetric energy density) becomes:

$$E_{vol} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h_s \cdot t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** When the question says "energy density" without specifying area or volume, default to the **volumetric form** $P/(v \cdot h_s \cdot t)$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A SLA machine uses a UV laser of power **$P = 200$ mW**, scan speed **$v = 500$ mm/s**, hatch spacing **$h_s = 0.1$ mm**, and layer thickness **$t = 0.05$ mm**. The resin has penetration depth **$D_p = 150$ μm** and critical energy **$E_c = 9$ mJ/cm²**. Calculate the cure depth $C_d$.

**Step 1 — Compute energy density $E_0$:**

$$E_0 = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h_s \cdot t} = \dfrac{200 \text{ mW}}{500 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.1 \text{ mm} \times 0.05 \text{ mm}}$$

$$\boxed{E_0 = \dfrac{200}{2.5} = 80 \text{ mJ/cm}^2}$$

> **Unit sanity check:** (mW·s) / (mm²) → 200 × 10⁻³ W × (1/500) s / (5 × 10⁻³ mm²) → all clean once standard units are used. For KTU exam, the above simplified form is acceptable.

**Step 2 — Apply Jacobs' cure-depth equation:**

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right) = 150 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{80}{9}\right) \text{ μm}$$

$$C_d = 150 \cdot \ln(8.888) = 150 \cdot 2.184 = 327.6 \text{ μm}$$

$$\boxed{C_d \approx 328 \text{ μm}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** The cure depth (328 μm) is **larger** than the layer thickness (50 μm), meaning the laser cures well through each layer and **bonds to the previous layer** — this is the desired condition. If $C_d < t$, inter-layer delamination will occur.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an SLA Machine (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Rationale:** The SLA process involves simultaneous coordination of optical, mechanical, chemical, and software sub-systems. The block diagram below illustrates the data-and-energy flow between them, which is the standard KTU illustration for full-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model STL File] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Layer Slices 25 to 100 microns]
    C --> D[Control Computer]
    D --> E1[Galvanometer X Y Driver]
    D --> E2[Z Stage Driver]
    D --> E3[Recoater Motor Driver]
    F[UV Laser Source 325 nm or 355 nm] --> G[Beam Expander]
    G --> H[Galvanometer Mirror X]
    H --> I[Galvanometer Mirror Y]
    I --> J[F Theta Lens]
    J --> K[Resin Vat Liquid Photopolymer]
    E1 --> H
    E1 --> I
    E2 --> L[Build Platform Z axis]
    E3 --> M[Recoater Blade]
    L --> K
    M --> K
    K --> N[Photo polymerized Layer]
    N --> O[3D Part Layer by Layer]
    O --> P[IPA Wash]
    P --> Q[UV Post Cure Chamber]
    Q --> R[Finished SLA Part]
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow Loop (Per-Layer Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start New Layer] --> S2[Recoat Resin]
    S2 --> S3[Galvos Trace 2D Cross Section]
    S3 --> S4[UV Laser Cures Resin]
    S4 --> S5{Z Equal to Top}
    S5 -- No --> S6[Move Platform by Delta Z]
    S6 --> S1
    S5 -- Yes --> S7[Detach Part from Platform]
    S7 --> S8[IPA Wash]
    S8 --> S9[UV Post Cure]
    S9 --> S10[Finished Part]
```

---

## 4.3 Free-Surface vs. Constrained-Surface — Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FreeSurface["Free Surface Bat Configuration Top Down"]
        FS1[UV Laser Above] --> FS2[Galvo Mirrors]
        FS2 --> FS3[Resin Vat with Open Top]
        FS3 --> FS4[Build Platform Dips from Top]
    end
    subgraph ConstrainedSurface["Constrained Surface Scroll Configuration Bottom Up"]
        CS1[UV Laser Below] --> CS2[Transparent Window FEP]
        CS2 --> CS3[Thin Resin Film]
        CS3 --> CS4[Build Platform Rises Upward]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** If the question asks for a "neat sketch of SLA", draw a labelled cross-section showing **laser → galvo mirrors → lens → resin vat → build platform**, with arrows indicating the **Z-movement direction** and laser path.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modelled on **actual KTU University End-Semester Examinations** under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme. Mark distribution, choice pattern, and Bloom's-level tagging follow KTU board conventions strictly.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define Stereo Lithography (SLA). Mention any two advantages of the process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Stereo Lithography (SLA) is a **vat-photopolymerization** based additive manufacturing process in which a **UV laser** is used to selectively cure (harden) a liquid photopolymer resin in a point-by-point and layer-by-layer manner to build a 3D part directly from a CAD model. It was invented by **Chuck Hull in 1986** and commercialized by 3D Systems.

> **Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. **Excellent surface finish** — Ra as low as 0.5 μm achievable, suitable for aesthetic and optical parts.
> 2. **High dimensional accuracy** — ±0.1 mm typical tolerance, best-in-class among AM technologies.
> 3. **Fine feature resolution** — capable of features smaller than 50 μm, ideal for jewelry and dental.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**List the four major components of an SLA machine and state the function of the galvanometer mirrors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Four major components (2 marks):**
1. **Resin Vat** — holds the liquid photopolymer.
2. **Build Platform** — provides the substrate and moves in Z.
3. **UV Laser Source** — supplies the curing radiation.
4. **Galvanometer Mirrors & F-Theta Lens** — direct and focus the laser beam.

**Function of Galvanometer Mirrors (1 mark):**
> The two **galvanometer (galvo) mirrors** rotate at very high speeds (up to several kHz) to **deflect the UV laser beam along the X and Y axes** of the resin surface, enabling the laser to trace the 2D cross-sectional scan path of each layer at high speed (without moving the heavy laser source physically).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part-B questions carry **internal choice** — students must answer **EITHER Question A OR Question B**. Each part-question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Explain in detail the Stereo Lithography (SLA) process with a neat labelled block diagram. Discuss the differences between the *Free-Surface* and *Constrained-Surface* configurations.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Working Principle & Process Flow

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
> SLA is a liquid-resin-based AM process that uses **photopolymerization** triggered by a **UV laser** to build parts layer by layer from a CAD model. It was developed by **Chuck Hull (1986)** and is the **first commercialized AM technology** in the world.

**Step-by-step process (4 marks):**
1. The 3D CAD model is converted to **STL format** and sliced into horizontal layers of thickness 0.025–0.1 mm.
2. The build platform is positioned at the top of the resin vat (free-surface) or just below the resin surface (constrained-surface).
3. A **recoater blade** deposits a fresh, uniform film of liquid photopolymer resin over the working surface.
4. The **UV laser** (He-Cd at 325 nm or frequency-tripled Nd:YVO₄ at 355 nm) is steered by **two galvanometer mirrors** through an **F-Theta lens**, focusing it to a small spot (~100–250 μm).
5. The laser traces the **2D cross-section** of the current layer. Wherever the laser hits, the **photoinitiator** molecules generate free radicals that convert the **monomers into a cross-linked solid polymer**.
6. After each layer, the platform moves down (or up) by exactly one **layer thickness**, and the cycle repeats.
7. After completion, the part is removed, **washed in IPA** to remove uncured resin, and **post-cured in a UV oven** to achieve full mechanical properties.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
> Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.1** above. Students drawing a hand-sketched diagram of `Laser → Galvo mirrors → F-Theta lens → Resin vat → Build platform` with all parts labelled will get full 2 marks.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Free-Surface vs. Constrained-Surface

**Model Answer (Comparison Table — 5 marks + Conclusion 2 marks):**

| S. No. | Feature | Free-Surface (Bat) | Constrained-Surface (Scroll) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Build platform position** | Above the resin, dips down | Below the resin, rises up |
| 2 | **Laser entry direction** | Top → downward into resin | Bottom → upward through transparent window |
| 3 | **Oxygen exposure** | High (open surface) | Negligible (sandwiched) |
| 4 | **Cure inhibition** | Significant oxygen inhibition | Minimal |
| 5 | **Part separation force** | Lower (gravity-assisted) | Higher (adhesion to window) |
| 6 | **Resin waste** | Higher (large vat fill) | Lower (small fill volume) |
| 7 | **Accuracy & finish** | Lower | Higher (preferred for desktop & dental) |
| 8 | **Examples** | SLA-250, SLA-500 (3D Systems vintage) | Form 2, Form 3 (Formlabs), most modern desktop SLA |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
> The **constrained-surface (bottom-up) configuration** is now industry-standard for modern desktop SLA printers because it **eliminates oxygen inhibition**, **reduces resin consumption**, and **delivers higher accuracy** than the original free-surface design. However, free-surface systems still dominate **large-format industrial SLA** (e.g., 3D Systems' SLA 7500) where part size matters more than resin economy.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Discuss the photopolymerization reaction mechanism in SLA. Derive the Jacobs' cure-depth equation and explain how the four process parameters (P, v, h, t) affect the build quality.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Photochemistry of Curing

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of Photo-Polymerization (5 marks):**
> The curing reaction in SLA is a **free-radical chain-growth polymerization** that proceeds in three classical stages:

**Stage 1 — Initiation:** A UV photon strikes a **photoinitiator (PI)** molecule (e.g., BAPO / TPO), causing it to cleave homolytically and produce **two free radicals (R•)**.

$$\text{PI} \xrightarrow{h\nu} 2\,\text{R}^{\bullet}$$

**Stage 2 — Propagation:** Each radical attacks the **C=C double bond** of a monomer (e.g., acrylate) and opens it, creating a new radical at the chain end. This new radical attacks another monomer — the chain grows rapidly.

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + M \longrightarrow \text{R–M}^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{+M} \text{R–M–M}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \cdots$$

**Stage 3 — Termination:** Two growing chains combine, or a chain transfers its radical to another molecule, stopping the reaction. The result is a **cross-linked solid polymer network**.

**Energy Threshold (2 marks):**
> Polymerization occurs only when the local energy density exceeds a **critical value $E_c$**, which depends on photoinitiator concentration, monomer reactivity, and resin formulation. Below $E_c$, no reaction occurs.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of Jacobs' Equation + Parameter Effects

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** See the complete step-by-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Final form:

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right), \quad \text{where} \quad E_0 = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h_s \cdot t}$$

**Effect of Parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on $C_d$ | Effect on Build Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **P (power) ↑** | $C_d$ increases (logarithmic) | Faster build, but **over-cure & loss of accuracy** |
| **v (scan speed) ↑** | $E_0$ decreases → $C_d$ decreases | Sharper features, but **slower build** |
| **$h_s$ (hatch spacing) ↑** | $E_0$ decreases → $C_d$ decreases | **Faster build, but weaker parts and possible voids** |
| **t (layer thickness) ↑** | $E_0$ decreases → $C_d$ decreases | **Faster build, but visible stair-stepping** |

> **Rule of thumb:** Always ensure $C_d \ge t$ to guarantee **inter-layer bonding** (no delamination).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "stereo" with "stereo audio":** Spelling it as *stereo-lithography* with a hyphen is acceptable; do NOT confuse with *stereo lithography* meaning "sound-based".
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the photoinitiator:** Many students describe the photopolymerization reaction but **omit the photoinitiator** — examiners specifically look for the 3-step mechanism (Initiation → Propagation → Termination).
> 3. **Wrong units in cure-depth formula:** $C_d$ and $D_p$ must be in **the same units** (μm or mm). Mixing up units costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing a proper diagram:** In 14-mark questions, a **labelled block diagram is compulsory** (2 marks reserved). A vague arrow sketch loses marks.
> 5. **Confusing SLS with SLA:** *Selective Laser Sintering* (SLS, powder-based) is fundamentally different from *Stereolithography* (SLA, resin-based). Do NOT mix them in the exam.
> 6. **Forgetting post-curing:** SLA parts require **IPA wash + UV post-cure** to reach full strength. Many students skip this step.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-density, exam-ready summary — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Full form of SLA:** Stereo Lithography Apparatus / Stereolithography — the world's **first commercialized AM process (1986, Chuck Hull, 3D Systems)**.
- ✅ **AM Category:** Vat Photopolymerization (ISO/ASTM 52900).
- ✅ **Energy source:** UV laser — typically **He-Cd (325 nm)** or **Nd:YVO₄ frequency-tripled (355 nm)**.
- ✅ **Starting material:** Liquid **photopolymer resin** (acrylates, epoxides) with photoinitiators.
- ✅ **Two configurations:** **Free-surface (Bat)** — top-down, vintage; **Constrained-surface (Scroll)** — bottom-up, modern desktop.
- ✅ **Cure-depth equation (most important formula):**
$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$
- ✅ **Energy-density formula:**
$$E_0 = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h_s \cdot t}$$
- ✅ **Four process knobs:** Laser Power (**P**), Scan Speed (**v**), Hatch Spacing (**$h_s$**), Layer Thickness (**t**).
- ✅ **Key components (always mention in 7-mark Q):** Resin vat, build platform, UV laser, galvanometer mirrors, F-Theta lens, recoater blade, control system.
- ✅ **Photochemistry:** Free-radical **chain-growth polymerization** — Initiation → Propagation → Termination.
- ✅ **Critical condition for good parts:** $C_d \ge t$ (cure depth must exceed layer thickness for inter-layer bonding).
- ✅ **Post-processing:** IPA wash → UV post-cure in oven → support removal.
- ✅ **Major applications:** Jewelry patterns, dental crowns, hearing aids, optical prototypes, investment casting patterns.
- ✅ **Key advantages:** Highest accuracy & surface finish among all AM technologies.
- ✅ **Key disadvantages:** Limited to **photopolymers only**; resin is **toxic & light-sensitive**; requires **support structures**; **post-curing mandatory**.
- ✅ **Common confusion to AVOID:** SLA ≠ SLS. SLA uses **liquid resin + UV laser**; SLS uses **powder + IR laser**.

> **End of Notes — Good luck with your KTU examination!** 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Light Processing (DLP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET616 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Light Processing (DLP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Light Processing (DLP)** is a vat photopolymerization-based additive manufacturing process in which an entire cross-sectional layer of a photopolymer resin is selectively cured (solidified) in a single exposure event by projecting a two-dimensional **masked image of ultraviolet (UV) or visible light** onto the resin surface, using a **Digital Micromirror Device (DMD)** or a **Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)** as the dynamic pattern generator.

The process belongs to the ASTM/ISO **52900** category of *VAT Photopolymerization*, sitting alongside Stereolithography (SLA). The defining technical differentiator of DLP is that **every voxel in a single layer is polymerized simultaneously**, rather than sequentially traced by a laser spot.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Flashlight Photograph" Metaphor

Imagine a dark room containing a tray of wet white paint (the liquid resin). Now imagine a giant overhead projector (the DLP engine) that can flash a *single still photograph* of a cross-sectional slice of an object (e.g., a heart shape). Where the photograph is **bright**, the paint instantly hardens; where the photograph is **dark**, the paint stays liquid. The tray is then lowered by exactly the thickness of the hardened skin, a new wet layer flows over it, and the *next* photograph is flashed. Repeat this **thousands of times** — layer by layer — and a complete 3D object emerges from the paint.

That is DLP: it is essentially a **pixel-wise, layer-wise "photographic printing" of solid objects from liquid plastic, one flash of light at a time**.

### 1.3 Key System Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Wavelength of curing light** | **365 – 405 nm** (UV-A / near-UV) | Matches photoinitiator absorption peaks |
| **Pixel size (XY resolution)** | **25 – 75 µm** | Governs lateral feature fidelity |
| **Layer thickness (Z resolution)** | **25 – 100 µm** | Governs stair-stepping & build time |
| **Build envelope (typical desktop)** | **192 × 120 × 200 mm³** | Industrial units reach 300+ mm |
| **DMD mirror count** | **up to 4 million micro-mirrors** | Each mirror = 1 pixel |
| **Resin viscosity (target)** | **< 1 Pa·s at 25 °C** | Determines recoating quality |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently award marks for stating that DLP uses a **DMD chip** (developed by Texas Instruments) as its spatial light modulator. Memorise this — it is a recurring 2-mark item.

### 1.4 Taxonomy of DLP Hardware Architectures

- **Top-down (bottom-up inverted) DLP** – Light projects *downward* onto resin through a transparent window; build platform lifts upward. (Most common in industrial systems; lower peel forces, higher accuracy.)
- **Bottom-up DLP** – Light projects *upward* from below the vat; build platform descends into the vat. (Common in desktop/maker systems.)
- **Continuous DLP (cDLP / CLIP-like)** – Oxygen-permeable membrane creates a "dead zone" enabling continuous (non-stepwise) vertical build.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of DLP — Step-by-Step

The DLP build sequence can be decomposed into **six discrete stages** per layer. Understanding the *why* of each step is the difference between rote memorisation and true KTU-ready mastery.

1. **Pre-build setup** — A 3D CAD model is sliced into N horizontal cross-sections of uniform thickness $\Delta z$ (the *layer height*). Each slice is converted into a 2D bitmap of pixels.
2. **Resin fill & platform positioning** — The build platform is positioned at a precise distance $\Delta z$ below the resin surface (or above the window, depending on architecture), creating a **liquid film of thickness $\Delta z$**.
3. **Image projection** — The DMD chip tilts millions of micro-mirrors (~ ±12°). "ON" mirrors reflect UV light *toward* the vat; "OFF" mirrors reflect light *away* into a heat sink. This simultaneously forms the layer image on the resin surface.
4. **Photopolymerisation (cure)** — Photons strike photoinitiator molecules in the resin, generating free radicals that trigger **chain-growth polymerisation**. The exposed voxels solidify from the bottom of the film upward.
5. **Separation / Peel step** — The build platform moves to detach the cured layer from the vat window (or the previous layer). Bottom-up systems use a peeling mechanism to overcome adhesion.
6. **Z-translation & recoat** — The platform translates by exactly $\Delta z$, fresh resin flows in, and the cycle repeats for the next slice.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why project a *whole layer* at once?** → Because DMD chips can switch ~10,000 mirrors simultaneously per frame, layer cure time is **independent of cross-sectional area**. A 10 mm × 10 mm square cures in the same time as a 100 mm × 100 mm square — a massive speed advantage over point-scanned SLA.
- **Why use UV and not visible light?** → UV wavelengths (365 – 405 nm) align with the absorption peaks of **radical photoinitiators** such as TPO (diphenyl(2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl)phosphine oxide) and BAPO, maximising photon-to-radical conversion efficiency.
- **Why need a peel step?** → Cured photopolymer adheres to the transparent window via van der Waals and chemical forces. Without separation, the next layer cannot recoat properly, and the part may crack.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all equations** the KTU examiner can realistically test on DLP. Master every row.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbol Definitions | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_d = D_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)$ — **Jacobs Working Curve** | $C_d$ = cure depth, $D_p$ = penetration depth, $E$ = exposure energy at surface, $E_c$ = critical energy to initiate cure | $C_d, D_p$ in µm; $E, E_c$ in mJ/cm² | Predicts how deep a given UV dose cures into the resin — fundamental DLP model |
| 2 | $E = I \cdot t$ | $I$ = incident irradiance, $t$ = exposure time | $E$ in mJ/cm², $I$ in mW/cm², $t$ in s | Calculates the UV dose delivered per layer |
| 3 | $P_{xy} = \dfrac{A_{proj}}{N_{pixels}}$ | $P_{xy}$ = pixel size, $A_{proj}$ = projection area on resin, $N_{pixels}$ = number of DMD pixels across the image | µm | Determines the smallest lateral feature that can be cured |
| 4 | $V_{voxel} = P_{xy} \cdot P_{xy} \cdot C_d$ | $V_{voxel}$ = volume of a single cured voxel | µm³ | Quantifies build-rate per pixel |
| 5 | $t_{layer} = t_{cure} + t_{peel} + t_{recoat}$ | $t_{layer}$ = total time per layer | s | Used in build-time estimation |
| 6 | $T_{build} = N_{layers} \cdot t_{layer} = \dfrac{H}{\Delta z} \cdot t_{layer}$ | $H$ = part height, $\Delta z$ = layer thickness | s, h | Predicts total manufacturing time |
| 7 | $R_{cure}(\%) = \dfrac{C_d}{\Delta z} \times 100$ | $R_{cure}$ = over-cure ratio | % | Controls z-resolution and inter-layer adhesion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Pitfall:** Inside any markdown table, the absolute value / vertical bar $\vert \cdot \vert$ **must** be written as `\vert` or `\mid` — never as a literal pipe character `|`. The pipe character breaks the markdown table parser. The KTU PDF converter used by the university is unforgiving on this.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dental & orthodontic industry** — DLP produces surgical guides, aligner models, and crowns with sub-50 µm accuracy, in under 30 minutes per part.
- **Jewellery casting patterns** — DLP's smooth surface finish (Ra 0.4 – 1.6 µm) eliminates post-machining before investment casting.
- **Microfluidics & MEMS** — Sub-50 µm XY resolution enables functional lab-on-chip devices.
- **Biomedical scaffolds** — Biocompatible photopolymers (e.g., PEGDA, methacrylated gelatin) cured via DLP yield precise pore architectures for tissue engineering.
- **Aerospace & automotive prototyping** — High-temperature resins (e.g., ceramic-filled photopolymers) yield functional prototypes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: The Jacobs Working Curve ($C_d$ Equation)

The **Jacobs equation** is the single most important analytical model in DLP. It predicts *how far light penetrates* into a photopolymer resin before it can no longer sustain polymerisation. The derivation proceeds from the physics of photon absorption.

**Step 1 — Start from the Beer–Lambert Law of light attenuation in a resin of molar absorptivity $\varepsilon$, photoinitiator concentration $[PI]$, and depth $z$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I(z) &= I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon \, [PI] \, z} \\
\end{aligned}$$

where $I_0$ is the surface irradiance and $I(z)$ is the irradiance remaining at depth $z$ below the resin surface.

**Step 2 — Convert the base-10 form to natural logarithm using $10^x = e^{x \ln 10}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I(z) &= I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon \, [PI] \, \ln(10) \, z} \\
     &= I_0 \cdot e^{-z \,/\, D_p} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Define the penetration depth $D_p$ as the inverse of the effective absorption coefficient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_p \equiv \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon \, [PI] \, \ln(10)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

This is the depth at which irradiance falls to $1/e$ (~36.8%) of its surface value. It is a **material constant** for a given resin and wavelength.

**Step 4 — Express the radiant exposure (energy density) at depth $z$ as $E(z) = I(z) \cdot t$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E(z) &= I_0 \, t \cdot e^{-z/D_p} \\
     &= E_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p} \\
\end{aligned}$$

where $E_0 = I_0 \cdot t$ is the surface exposure.

**Step 5 — Apply the *cure criterion*: polymerisation stops when $E(z)$ falls below the critical energy $E_c$ required to initiate gelation. The cure depth $C_d$ is therefore the depth at which $E(z) = E_c$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_c &= E_0 \cdot e^{-C_d \,/\, D_p} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Solve algebraically for $C_d$ by taking the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(E_c) &= \ln(E_0) - \dfrac{C_d}{D_p} \\
\dfrac{C_d}{D_p} &= \ln(E_0) - \ln(E_c) \\
\dfrac{C_d}{D_p} &= \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right) \\
C_d &= D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right) \\
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result — Jacobs Working Curve:** $\boxed{\,C_d = D_p \,\ln(E_0 \,/\, E_c)\,}$
>
> *Interpretation:* To **increase cure depth** for a given resin, increase surface exposure $E_0$ (longer exposure or higher lamp power). The ratio $E_0/E_c$ is the **over-exposure factor**; it must exceed 1, otherwise no curing occurs.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU-Style Calculation

**Problem (calibrated to Part A / 3-mark level):** A DLP system uses a resin with penetration depth $D_p = 0.15 \text{ mm}$ and critical energy $E_c = 8 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$. The UV projector delivers an irradiance $I = 12 \text{ mW/cm}^2$ for an exposure time $t = 6 \text{ s}$ per layer. Calculate the cure depth $C_d$ and determine whether a 50 µm layer can be fully cured.

**Step 1 — Compute the surface exposure $E_0$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= I \cdot t \\
    &= 12 \;\text{mW/cm}^2 \times 6 \;\text{s} \\
    &= 72 \;\text{mJ/cm}^2 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Jacobs equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_d &= D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right) \\
    &= 0.15 \;\text{mm} \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{72}{8}\right) \\
    &= 0.15 \;\text{mm} \times \ln(9) \\
    &= 0.15 \;\text{mm} \times 2.1972 \\
    &= 0.3296 \;\text{mm} \\
    &= 329.6 \;\mu\text{m} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to the layer thickness $\Delta z = 50 \;\mu\text{m}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{C_d}{\Delta z} &= \dfrac{329.6}{50} \approx 6.6 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Since $C_d \gg \Delta z$, the resin is **vastly over-cured**. To improve z-resolution, the operator should *reduce* $E_0$ (lower $I$ or shorter $t$) until $C_d \approx 50$ µm. Solving $C_d = 50$ µm for $E_0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_0^{\text{ideal}} &= E_c \cdot e^{C_d / D_p} \\
                   &= 8 \cdot e^{50/150} \\
                   &= 8 \cdot e^{0.333} \\
                   &= 8 \cdot 1.3956 \\
                   &\approx 11.16 \;\text{mJ/cm}^2 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Corresponding exposure time at $I = 12$ mW/cm²:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{ideal}} &= \dfrac{E_0^{\text{ideal}}}{I} = \dfrac{11.16}{12} \approx 0.93 \;\text{s} \\
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Real DLP systems typically *deliberately over-cure* by 20 – 50% (i.e., $C_d = 1.2\,\Delta z$ to $1.5\,\Delta z$) to ensure **inter-layer adhesion**. Under-curing causes delamination; over-curing reduces z-resolution. The Jacobs equation is the analytical lever for balancing this trade-off.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DLP vs. SLA vs. LCD

| Parameter | DLP (DMD) | SLA (Laser Scan) | mSLA (LCD Masking) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Light source | Arc lamp + DMD | UV laser (Nd:YVO₄) | UV LED array + LCD |
| Curing strategy | **Whole layer / frame** | Point-by-point scan | Whole layer / frame |
| XY resolution | 25 – 75 µm | 25 – 50 µm (laser spot) | 35 – 50 µm (LCD pixel) |
| Speed (relative) | **High** | Low – Medium | High |
| Pixel contrast | Excellent (binary DMD) | N/A (continuous) | Moderate (LCD light leakage) |
| Cost | High (DMD chip) | Very high (laser) | Low – Medium |
| Light uniformity | Excellent | N/A | Reduced at panel edges |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 DLP System Architecture — Functional Block Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a top-down industrial DLP system, including the optical, mechanical, and material-flow subsystems. It is rendered as a flow chart because true 3D physical drawings cannot be generated within this text-based system, but the diagram below gives the examiner-ready, complete systems view of the technology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model Input STL or OBJ Format] --> A2[Slicer Software Layer Slicing Engine]
    A2 --> A3[2D Bitmap Stack Per Layer Image Generation]
    A3 --> A4[Video Signal HDMI to DMD Controller]
    A4 --> B1[UV Light Source LED or Mercury Arc 365 to 405 nm]
    B1 --> B2[Condenser Optics Collimation Lens]
    B2 --> B3[Digital Micromirror Device DMD Chip Million Mirrors]
    A4 --> B3
    B3 --> B4[Projection Lens Reduction Optics]
    B4 --> B5[Resin Vat Transparent FEP or PDMS Window]
    B5 --> C1[Photopolymer Resin Tank Photoinitiator plus Monomer]
    B1 -.UV photons.-> B5
    B5 --> C2[Cured Layer Solidified Voxels]
    C2 --> D1[Build Platform Z Axis Linear Stage]
    D1 --> D2[Z Separation and Peel Step]
    D2 --> D3[Recoat Fresh Resin Layer Forms]
    D3 --> B5
    D1 --> E1[Finished 3D Part Layer Stack]
    E1 --> F1[Post Processing IPA Wash]
    F1 --> F2[Secondary UV Cure Chamber 405 nm Flood]
    F2 --> F3[Final Cured Functional Part]
    C1 --> B5
    style B3 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B5 fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C2 fill:#B0E0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F3 fill:#FFB0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The top row (A1–A4) is the **digital image generation pipeline** (software). The middle block (B1–B5) is the **optical engine** — note that the DMD (highlighted amber) is the *heart* of the system. The lower block (C1–D3) is the **photopolymerisation & Z-translation loop**. The final block (E1–F3) is the **post-processing chain**.

---

### 4.2 DLP Process Flow — Single Layer Curing Cycle

The next diagram isolates the **per-layer curing sequence**, which is the most commonly tested cycle in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1([Start of Layer n]) --> S2[Platform positioned at z equals n times delta z]
    S2 --> S3[Resin recoats build area as a thin film of thickness delta z]
    S3 --> S4[DMD mirrors tilt to form bitmap image of slice n]
    S4 --> S5[UV exposure for time t equals E over I]
    S5 --> S6[Photopolymerisation of exposed voxels]
    S6 --> S7[Peel step separation of cured layer from window]
    S7 --> S8{Z equals Z final}
    S8 -- No --> S2
    S8 -- Yes --> S9([End of Build])
    style S4 fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S6 fill:#B0E0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S7 fill:#FFB0B0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic of the Jacobs Working Curve (Conceptual)

A schematic of the **log-linear cure-depth relationship** is shown below. This is a verbal description, as the curve itself is a standard textbook plot: the x-axis is $\ln(E_0 / E_c)$ (dimensionless), the y-axis is $C_d$ in µm. The slope is $D_p$, and the x-intercept is at $E_0 = E_c$ (cure threshold).

> **Key visual takeaway for exams:** The Jacobs curve is a **straight line in semi-log coordinates**. The intercept on the energy axis gives $E_c$ (critical / threshold energy). The slope gives $D_p$ (penetration depth). DLP operators tune $E_0$ upward along the x-axis to increase $C_d$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]
> **Differentiate between Stereolithography (SLA) and Digital Light Processing (DLP) as additive manufacturing processes. Mention the role of the Digital Micromirror Device.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SLA | DLP |
|---|---|---|
| Light source | UV laser (point source) | UV lamp + DMD (area source) |
| Curing strategy | **Point-by-point scanning** of each layer | **Whole layer cured simultaneously** via projected image |
| Resolution control | Laser spot size (~25 – 50 µm) | DMD pixel size (~25 – 75 µm) |
| Speed | Slow (scanning-dependent) | **Fast** (area-independent) |
| Cost | Higher (laser, galvo) | Lower per unit area (DMD) |

**Role of DMD (1 mark):** The Digital Micromirror Device is an array of **micron-sized aluminium mirrors** (developed by Texas Instruments) that can be individually tilted between "ON" and "OFF" states. In "ON" state the mirror reflects UV light *through* the projection lens onto the resin; in "OFF" state it reflects light *away* into a heat sink. The DMD thus acts as a **dynamic, high-speed binary spatial light modulator**, generating the per-layer cross-sectional image of the part.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]
> **State the Jacobs Working Curve equation. Explain the significance of the penetration depth $D_p$ and critical energy $E_c$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Jacobs working curve is given by:

$$C_d = D_p \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

- **Penetration depth $D_p$** (1 mark): It is the **characteristic depth** at which the UV irradiance decays to $1/e$ (~36.8 %) of its surface value. It is governed by the photoinitiator concentration and molar absorptivity. A *larger* $D_p$ means the resin cures *deeper* per unit exposure — useful for thicker layers but reduces z-resolution.
- **Critical energy $E_c$** (1 mark): It is the **minimum exposure energy** required to initiate polymerisation (gel point). Below $E_c$ the resin remains liquid. $E_c$ is a *threshold* parameter and depends on photoinitiator chemistry and resin formulation.
- **Engineering use** (1 mark): By plotting $C_d$ vs $\ln(E_0)$, $D_p$ is the slope and $E_c$ is the x-intercept. The Jacobs equation is used to **calibrate DLP process parameters** for a given resin.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]**

### **(a)** Explain the principle of Digital Light Processing (DLP) for additive manufacturing. Describe the construction and working of a DLP system with the help of a labelled block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):** DLP is a vat photopolymerization process that solidifies photopolymer resin **layer by layer** by projecting UV images of cross-sections. Each entire layer is cured in **one exposure event**, distinguishing DLP from point-scanned SLA.

**Construction (3 marks):** A DLP system consists of:
- **UV light source** — typically a high-power LED array or mercury arc lamp emitting at 365 – 405 nm.
- **Digital Micromirror Device (DMD)** — a chip containing millions of micro-mirrors, each tiltable between ON and OFF positions.
- **Projection optics** — condenser, projection lens, and (in top-down systems) a redirection mirror.
- **Resin vat** — a tank with a transparent, oxygen-inert, non-stick bottom (FEP or PDMS film).
- **Build platform** — a vertically driven Z-stage that holds and lifts the growing part.
- **Controller** — synchronises DMD pattern, UV exposure, and Z motion.

**Working (2 marks):** The sliced 2D image of each layer is sent to the DMD. UV light reflected from "ON" mirrors passes through the projection optics and strikes the resin, curing the exposed pattern in one frame. The platform separates, translates by $\Delta z$, and the next layer is exposed. The process repeats until the build is complete. Post-processing involves IPA washing and a secondary UV flood cure.

### **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the Jacobs working curve model for DLP. A photopolymer resin has $D_p = 0.20 \text{ mm}$ and $E_c = 12 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$. If the projected irradiance is $20 \text{ mW/cm}^2$ and exposure time is 5 s, compute the cure depth and the over-cure ratio for a layer thickness of 60 µm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Jacobs Working Curve (3 marks):**

The Jacobs model relates the cure depth $C_d$ to the exposure $E_0$ through:

$$C_d = D_p \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

- $C_d$ = cure depth (µm)
- $D_p$ = penetration depth of the resin (µm)
- $E_0$ = exposure energy at the resin surface (mJ/cm²)
- $E_c$ = critical energy to initiate cure (mJ/cm²)

When plotted as $C_d$ vs $\ln(E_0)$, the graph is a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** = $D_p$ (resin-specific)
- **x-intercept** = $\ln(E_c)$, hence $E_c$ is the energy threshold below which no curing occurs.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the surface exposure $E_0$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= I \cdot t \\
    &= 20 \;\text{mW/cm}^2 \times 5 \;\text{s} \\
    &= 100 \;\text{mJ/cm}^2 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cure depth $C_d$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_d &= D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right) \\
    &= 0.20 \;\text{mm} \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{100}{12}\right) \\
    &= 200 \;\mu\text{m} \times \ln(8.333) \\
    &= 200 \;\mu\text{m} \times 2.1203 \\
    &= 424.06 \;\mu\text{m} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the over-cure ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{cure} &= \dfrac{C_d}{\Delta z} = \dfrac{424.06}{60} \\
          &\approx 7.07 \;\text{(or } 707\%\text{)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Inference (1 mark):** The cure depth of 424 µm is **~7× the layer thickness of 60 µm**, indicating severe over-curing. To achieve a target $C_d = 1.2 \cdot \Delta z = 72$ µm, the exposure should be reduced to:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_0^{\text{ideal}} &= E_c \cdot e^{C_d/D_p} \\
                   &= 12 \cdot e^{72/200} \\
                   &= 12 \cdot e^{0.36} \\
                   &= 12 \cdot 1.4333 \\
                   &\approx 17.2 \;\text{mJ/cm}^2 \\
\end{aligned}$$

i.e., exposure time $\approx 17.2 / 20 = 0.86$ s.

---

> **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Use this option if a student prefers a process-parameter/comparative question.*

### **(a)** Discuss the various process parameters that affect the quality of parts produced by Digital Light Processing. Explain how cure depth, exposure time, and layer thickness are interrelated using the Jacobs equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Key Process Parameters (4 marks):**
1. **Cure depth $C_d$** — must exceed layer thickness $\Delta z$ to ensure inter-layer bonding; typically $C_d = 1.2\,\Delta z$ to $1.5\,\Delta z$.
2. **Layer thickness $\Delta z$** — smaller layers → higher z-resolution and smoother surface, but longer build times (more layers).
3. **Exposure time $t$** — longer exposure increases $E_0$, hence $C_d$; affects both dimensional accuracy and build speed.
4. **Irradiance $I$** — depends on lamp power and projection optics; higher $I$ enables shorter exposures.
