<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Material Jetting. List any two of its unique advantages over other AM processes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Material Jetting is an Additive Manufacturing process (ASTM F2792 Category — MJ) in which **droplets of build material** are selectively jetted from a print head and **solidified (typically by UV photopolymerization)** to form a 3D part layer-by-layer.

**Two Unique Advantages (1 Mark):**
1. **Highest surface finish** among all AM processes (Ra ≈ 0.5–2 µm), eliminating post-finishing in many cases.
2. **Multi-material and full-colour capability** in a single build (up to 7 materials and 600,000+ colours in Stratasys J850).
3. *Any other valid advantage* (e.g., office-friendly, no powder handling, high XY resolution of ~20 µm).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the role of photoinitiators in Material Jetting photopolymer resins.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Photoinitiators (e.g., TPO, BAPO, Irgacure series) are **UV-sensitive molecules** added to the photopolymer resin. Upon absorbing a UV photon (365–405 nm), they undergo **α-cleavage** to generate **free radicals**. These radicals attack the **C=C double bonds** of acrylate/methacrylate monomers, initiating **chain-growth polymerization** that converts the liquid droplet into a solid voxel in under one second. The photoinitiator is the **molecular switch** that makes the otherwise-stable liquid resin solidify only where UV light is intentionally delivered.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question offers an **internal choice**. Both Question A and Question B below are **completely independent options** — only one needs to be answered, but a student may attempt both (best score retained).

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the **Material Jetting (PolyJet) process**. Highlight the role of the print head, UV lamp, build platform, and support material.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CAD & Slicing (1 Mark):** A 3D CAD model is exported as an STL file and sliced into horizontal layers of thickness $\Delta z$ (typically 15.8 to 32 µm). The slicing software (e.g., **PolyJet Studio** or **Objet Studio**) also generates support-material geometry for all overhangs and enclosed volumes.

**Step 2 — Jetting of Build Material (2 Marks):** The print head assembly — comprising **8 to 16 inkjet-style heads** with up to **1536 nozzles per head** — raster-scans over the build platform. **Piezoelectric actuators** eject **photopolymer droplets** of 5–85 pL volume from nozzles of 25–50 µm diameter, in response to voltage pulses of 80–120 V. Droplet positioning accuracy is ±5–10 µm.

**Step 3 — UV Curing (2 Marks):** Immediately after jetting, **high-intensity UV lamps** (mercury-arc or UV-LED, 365–405 nm) flood the freshly deposited droplets, causing **free-radical photopolymerization** in under 1 second. The Jacobs cure-depth relationship $C_d = D_p \ln(E/E_c)$ governs the polymerized layer thickness.

**Step 4 — Support Material (1 Mark):** A second set of print heads jets a **sacrificial support material** (wax-based or water-soluble gel) at overhangs > 45°, internal lattices, and bridge undersides.

**Step 5 — Z-Step & Repeat (1 Mark):** The build platform lowers by $\Delta z$ and the cycle repeats. After build completion, supports are removed by **water-jetting** or **NaOH dissolution**, followed by optional **UV post-cure** for full polymerization.

---

**(b)** A photopolymer resin has $D_p = 50$ µm and $E_c = 10$ mJ/cm². The installed UV source delivers $E = 80$ mJ/cm² at the build-platform surface. The process uses a layer thickness of $\Delta z = 0.028$ mm. **Determine:**
   (i) The cured depth $C_d$, and
   (ii) Whether the layer thickness is adequately cured with a safety margin.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $D_p = 50$ µm
- $E_c = 10$ mJ/cm²
- $E = 80$ mJ/cm²
- $\Delta z = 0.028$ mm $= 28$ µm

**Part (i) — Cured Depth Calculation (3 Marks):**

Apply Jacobs' equation:

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_c}\right)$$

$$C_d = 50 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{80}{10}\right) = 50 \cdot \ln(8)$$

$$\ln(8) = 2.0794$$

$$C_d = 50 \cdot 2.0794 = 103.97 \approx 104 \text{ µm}$$

**Part (ii) — Adequacy Check (3 Marks):**

For robust inter-layer adhesion, the standard design rule is $C_d \geq 2 \times \Delta z$:

$$2 \times \Delta z = 2 \cdot 28 = 56 \text{ µm}$$

Since $C_d = 104$ µm $> 56$ µm, the condition is **satisfied with a safety factor**:

$$SF = \frac{C_d}{2\Delta z} = \frac{104}{56} = 1.86$$

**Conclusion:** The UV dose is **adequate** with a safety margin of **1.86**. The next 28 µm of resin beneath the cured layer is partially polymerized, ensuring strong inter-layer bonding.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]`

**(a)** Compare **Material Jetting** with **Stereolithography (SLA)** under the following heads: build principle, resolution, surface finish, multi-material capability, support material, and typical applications.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Material Jetting | Stereolithography (SLA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Build Principle (1 Mark)** | Droplet jetting + UV cure (point-wise) | Laser scanning + UV cure (volumetric) |
| **XY Resolution (1 Mark)** | 20–85 µm (multi-head) | 25–140 µm (laser spot size) |
| **Z Resolution (1 Mark)** | 15–32 µm | 20–50 µm |
| **Surface Finish (1 Mark)** | **Ra 0.5–2 µm** (best in class) | Ra 1–5 µm |
| **Multi-Material (1 Mark)** | **Yes — up to 7 materials, 600k+ colours** | No (single vat, single resin) |
| **Support Material (1 Mark)** | Wax/gel, water-soluble | Same resin, manually designed scaffolds |
| **Applications (1 Mark)** | Visual prototypes, dental/medical, investment casting, soft-touch parts | Functional prototypes, jewellery masters, dental models |

---

**(b)** For a Material Jetting system with **8 effective print heads**, each firing at **30 kHz** with a droplet volume of **30 pL**, calculate the **volumetric deposition rate $Q$** in mm³/s. Also estimate the **time to deposit one layer** of a 90 × 60 mm part at $\Delta z = 0.0276$ mm.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Volumetric Deposition Rate $Q$ (3 Marks):**

Convert units: $V_d = 30$ pL $= 30 \times 10^{-9}$ cm³ $= 30 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^{3}$ mm³ $= 3 \times 10^{-5}$ mm³

$$Q = N \cdot V_d \cdot f$$

$$Q = 8 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mm}^3 \cdot 30{,}000 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$Q = 8 \cdot 3 \cdot 30{,}000 \times 10^{-5} = 7.2 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Part 2 — Layer Volume (2 Marks):**

$$V_{layer} = L \cdot W \cdot \Delta z = 90 \times 60 \times 0.0276 = 149.04 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Part 3 — Time to Deposit One Layer (2 Marks):**

$$t_{jet} = \frac{V_{layer}}{Q} = \frac{149.04}{7.2} = 20.7 \text{ s}$$

**Conclusion:** It takes approximately **20.7 seconds** to jet one layer of the 90 × 60 mm part — which, when combined with the scan and cure times, defines the layer cycle time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing layer thickness ($\Delta z$) with cure depth ($C_d$):** $\Delta z$ is the *Z-step increment*; $C_d$ is the *polymerized depth* calculated from Jacobs' equation. They are NOT the same. Marks deducted for using one in the other's formula.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion for pL → mm³:** 1 pL $= 10^{-9}$ cm³ $= 10^{-6}$ mm³. Many students write $10^{-9}$ mm³ — losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the support material in the build cycle:** Examiners specifically test if students can name the sacrificial support as a critical subsystem of MJ.
> 4. **Writing "Material Jetting = Inkjet Printing":** Material Jetting is a 3D process, not 2D. Emphasize the **layer-by-layer build** and **photopolymerization** to earn full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the photoinitiator explanation:** Any question on Material Jetting must mention the **photoinitiator + free-radical mechanism**, otherwise 1–2 marks are lost.
> 6. **Not mentioning support material fraction in build cost:** Examiners often ask about material cost; include a support-fraction estimate.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU ESE Revision — Material Jetting in 60 Seconds**

- ☐ **ASTM Category:** Material Jetting (MJ), Code: MJ.
- ☐ **Definition:** Selective jetting of droplets + in-situ solidification (UV cure).
- ☐ **Commercial Names:** PolyJet (Stratasys), MultiJet Modeling (3D Systems), Drop-on-Demand.
- ☐ **Five-Stage Layer Cycle:** (1) Jet build material, (2) UV cure, (3) Jet support, (4) Optional planarization, (5) Z-step down.
- ☐ **Droplet Volume:** 5–85 pL. **Nozzle Diameter:** 25–50 µm. **Actuation:** Piezoelectric.
- ☐ **Layer Thickness:** 15.8 µm (HQ), 27.6 µm (Std), 32 µm (HS).
- ☐ **Resolution:** XY ≈ 20–85 µm, Z = $\Delta z$.
- ☐ **Surface Finish:** Ra 0.5–2 µm — **best in class** for AM.
- ☐ **Multi-Material:** Up to 7 resins in one build; up to 600,000+ colour combinations.
- ☐ **Cure Equation (must memorize):** $C_d = D_p \ln(E/E_c)$.
- ☐ **Droplet Formation Equation:** $r_d = \left(\frac{3 r_n \gamma}{2\rho g}\right)^{1/3}$.
- ☐ **Hagen–Poiseuille:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^4}$ — used to predict nozzle clogging.
- ☐ **Build Time Formula:** $t_{build} = \dfrac{H}{\Delta z} \cdot t_{layer}$.
- ☐ **Support Material:** Wax-based or water-soluble gel; removed by water-jet or NaOH.
- ☐ **Applications:** Visual prototypes, dental/medical models, investment casting, soft-touch ergonomic testing.
- ☐ **Key Distinction vs. SLA:** MJ jettable, multi-material, full-colour; SLA is laser-scanned, single resin, no colour.
- ☐ **Key Distinction vs. FDM:** MJ droplets + UV cure; FDM continuous filament + thermal melt.
- ☐ **Key Failure Mode:** Nozzle clogging (because of sub-50 µm orifice and $r^4$ dependence in Hagen–Poiseuille).
- ☐ **Limitation:** Photopolymer degradation under prolonged UV/heat — not suitable for outdoor or high-temperature service.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Material Jetting Module Notes — KTU OEMET616 Module 2.**
> These notes are aligned to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's-Level Outcomes** for additive manufacturing: *Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate* — covering the full cognitive spectrum required for KTU ESE evaluation. Best of luck with your exam preparation! 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET616 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)** is a material-extrusion based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process in which a continuous filament of thermoplastic polymer is fed into a heated liquefier (hot-end), melted to a semi-liquid state, and extruded through a fine nozzle in a precisely controlled path to build a three-dimensional part layer-by-layer on a build platform, with each newly deposited layer fusing to the previous one through thermal bonding and subsequent solidification.

FDM was originally developed and patented by **Scott Crump** in **1989**, and subsequently commercialized by **Stratasys Inc.** It is also referred to as **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)** in the open-source / RepRap community. FDM is classified under the **ASTM F2792** standard under the category **"Material Extrusion"**.

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) places FDM as the **pioneering and most widely deployed** extrusion-based AM process, serving as the baseline reference for understanding other material-extrusion variants.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of FDM as a "Hot Glue Gun Mounted on a Plotter":** Imagine a hot glue gun whose tip moves precisely over a table (like an XY plotter). When you press the trigger, melted glue comes out and sticks to the table. Once the first outline is done, you fill it inside. Then the table drops a tiny bit, and you draw the next layer on top. Repeat this hundreds of times, and a 3D object rises from the table — exactly the same way a 3D printer works, except it uses engineering-grade plastic thread instead of glue sticks.

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU

- **Filament:** The raw thermoplastic feedstock, typically supplied as a spool of 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm diameter wire.
- **Liquefier / Hot-End:** The heated block (typically **200 °C – 260 °C**) where the filament melts.
- **Nozzle:** The orifice (typical diameter **0.2 mm – 1.0 mm**) through which molten material is extruded.
- **Build Platform / Bed:** The flat substrate (often heated to **50 °C – 110 °C**) on which the part is constructed.
- **Road / Bead:** A single extruded line of material forming part of one layer.
- **Raster:** The pattern of roads within a single layer (e.g., 0°, 90°, ±45°).
- **Layer Thickness (t):** The vertical height of each deposited layer, usually **0.05 mm – 0.3 mm**.

## 1.4 Core Engineering Significance

| Aspect | Typical Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | ± 0.2 % (lower bound ± 0.1 mm) |
| **Build Volume (Desktop)** | 200 × 200 × 200 mm to 300 × 300 × 400 mm |
| **Build Volume (Industrial)** | Up to 1000 × 1000 × 1000 mm |
| **Support Material** | Soluble (e.g., PVA, HIPS) or break-away |
| **Standard Classification** | ASTM F2792 – Material Extrusion |
| **Minimum Feature Size** | ≈ 2 × nozzle diameter |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | 6 – 15 µm (post-processed: < 1 µm) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The FDM process can be broken down into the following sequential operations:

1. **CAD to STL Conversion:** The 3D CAD model is tessellated into an **STL** file (stereolithography format), which approximates the geometry using a mesh of triangles.
2. **Slicing:** Specialized software (e.g., **Cura, Slic3r, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D**) slices the STL into horizontal cross-sections of thickness *t* and generates **G-code** (toolpath instructions).
3. **Filament Feeding:** A drive gear (extruder) pulls the thermoplastic filament from the spool and pushes it into the hot-end.
4. **Melting:** Inside the liquefier, the polymer is heated above its **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** and close to its **Melting Temperature (Tm)**. For example, PLA: Tg ≈ 60 °C, Tm ≈ 150 – 160 °C; ABS: Tg ≈ 105 °C, Tm ≈ 220 – 230 °C.
5. **Extrusion & Deposition:** The molten polymer is forced out through the nozzle as a thin road of width *W* and height *t*, following the toolpath.
6. **Layer Bonding:** Each fresh road is deposited adjacent to (and on top of) the previous one. **Thermal welding** occurs as the heat of the new road partially re-melts the top surface of the previous layer.
7. **Build Platform Movement:** After each layer, the build platform (Z-axis) lowers by *t*, and the next layer is deposited.
8. **Cooling & Solidification:** The part is gradually cooled (often aided by a part-cooling fan), with residual stresses managed to minimize warping.
9. **Post-Processing:** Support structures are removed, and the part may be sanded, polished, or chemically smoothed.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why heat the filament above Tg?** — Above Tg, polymer chains gain mobility, allowing them to inter-diffuse across the interface between two adjacent roads, creating a strong weld.
- **Why a heated bed?** — Maintains the bottom layers above Tg to reduce **warping** due to differential cooling.
- **Why a part-cooling fan?** — For materials like PLA, rapid solidification preserves dimensional accuracy. For ABS, fans are minimized to prevent layer delamination.
- **Why 45°/0°/90° raster patterns?** — Anisotropic strength is the Achilles' heel of FDM; the raster angle directly controls the **mechanical anisotropy** of the printed part.

## 2.3 Key Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Part |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer thickness | *t* | 0.05 – 0.3 mm | Lower = better resolution, longer build time |
| Nozzle temperature | *Tn* | 180 – 260 °C | Higher = better bonding, more stringing |
| Bed temperature | *Tb* | 50 – 110 °C | Higher = less warping |
| Print speed | *v* | 30 – 150 mm/s | Higher = faster, poorer surface |
| Raster angle | *θ* | 0° – 90° / ±45° | Directly controls strength anisotropy |
| Road width | *W* | 0.4 – 0.8 mm | Wider = faster, less detail |
| Infill density | *ρi* | 10 % – 100 % | Higher = stronger, heavier |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations appear frequently in KTU Part B (14-mark) and Part A (3-mark) questions on FDM. Memorize the relationships, not just the symbols.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = v_d \cdot W \cdot t$ | Volumetric flow rate of extrudate (where $v_d$ = deposition velocity, $W$ = road width, $t$ = layer thickness) | mm³/s |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = Q \cdot \rho$ | Mass flow rate ($\rho$ = density of molten polymer) | g/s |
| 3 | $Q = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$ | Volumetric flow rate expressed in terms of filament feed ($d_f$ = filament diameter, $v_f$ = filament feed velocity) | mm³/s |
| 4 | $T_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{Q} \cdot N_{layers}$ | Estimated build time (with $V_{part}$ = part volume, $N_{layers}$ = total number of layers) | s |
| 5 | $N_{layers} = \frac{H}{t}$ | Number of layers (H = total part height) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $v_d = v_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_f}{2r_n}\right)^2$ | Relationship between filament feed velocity and deposition velocity ($r_n$ = nozzle radius) | mm/s |
| 7 | $\sigma_{layer} = f(T_g, t, \rho_i, \theta)$ | Layer-bond strength as a function of material and process parameters (empirical) | MPa |
| 8 | $R_a \approx 0.5 \cdot t$ | Approximate surface roughness for side surfaces (≈ half of layer thickness) | µm |
| 9 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{STL}} \times 100\%$ | Volumetric efficiency of the printed part (considering infill) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical safeguard:** In all tables above, the vertical pipe `|` has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table parser — a non-negotiable formatting rule of KTU-premium notes.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rapid Prototyping:** Functional prototypes in 24–48 hours.
- **Jigs & Fixtures:** Custom tooling for manufacturing assembly lines.
- **Biomedical:** Patient-specific anatomical models, surgical guides, bioresorbable scaffolds (PCL, PLA).
- **Aerospace & Automotive:** Lightweight lattice structures, ducting, and custom interior components.
- **Architecture:** Scale models and form-finding mock-ups.
- **Education:** Affordable STEM teaching tool, ubiquitous in university maker-labs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Mass Continuity Equation (Relating Filament Feed to Extrudate Deposition)

This is the **single most important relationship** in FDM process analysis. It is derived from the principle of **conservation of mass** (incompressible melt assumption).

### Given:
- Filament diameter: $d_f$
- Filament feed velocity: $v_f$
- Nozzle (extrudate) diameter: $d_n$ (radius $r_n$)
- Deposition velocity (head movement in XY): $v_d$
- Road width: $W$
- Layer thickness: $t$

### Step 1: Volumetric flow rate of incoming filament
The filament is a cylinder of diameter $d_f$, hence its cross-sectional area is:

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4}$$

The volumetric flow rate of the filament entering the hot-end is:

$$Q_{in} = A_f \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$$

### Step 2: Volumetric flow rate of outgoing extrudate
The extrudate is deposited as a road of width $W$, thickness $t$, with the nozzle (and thus the road) being laid down at velocity $v_d$. The cross-sectional area of the road is:

$$A_{out} = W \cdot t$$

Therefore, the volumetric flow rate out is:

$$Q_{out} = A_{out} \cdot v_d = W \cdot t \cdot v_d$$

### Step 3: Apply conservation of volume (incompressible melt)
For an incompressible polymer melt in steady state:

$$Q_{in} = Q_{out}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f = W \cdot t \cdot v_d$$

### Step 4: Solve for the deposition velocity
The deposition velocity (i.e., the speed at which the nozzle must move in the XY plane for a given filament feed) is therefore:

$$v_d = \frac{\pi d_f^2 \cdot v_f}{4 \, W \, t}$$

Or, equivalently, the filament feed velocity required for a given head speed is:

$$v_f = \frac{4 \, W \, t \, v_d}{\pi d_f^2}$$

### Step 5: Compact form (using nozzle radius)
If we approximate the road width as equal to the nozzle diameter (a common assumption in FDM: $W \approx d_n = 2r_n$):

$$v_d = v_f \cdot \left(\frac{d_f}{2 r_n}\right)^2$$

This shows that the deposition velocity is **quadratically related** to the ratio of filament to nozzle diameter. A small change in nozzle size produces a large change in $v_d$.

### Numerical Example (KTU-Style):
> Given: $d_f = 1.75 \text{ mm}$, $r_n = 0.2 \text{ mm}$, $v_f = 5 \text{ mm/s}$, $W = 0.4 \text{ mm}$, $t = 0.2 \text{ mm}$. Find $v_d$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{in} &= \frac{\pi (1.75)^2}{4} \times 5 \\
&= \frac{\pi \times 3.0625}{4} \times 5 \\
&= 2.4053 \times 5 \\
&= 12.026 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_d &= \frac{Q_{in}}{W \cdot t} = \frac{12.026}{0.4 \times 0.2} \\
&= \frac{12.026}{0.08} \\
&= 150.33 \, \text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $v_d \approx 150.33 \text{ mm/s}$ — this is the maximum XY head speed the extruder can sustain.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Build Time Estimation

### Given:
- Part bounding box: $L \times B \times H$ (length × breadth × height)
- Infill density: $\rho_i$
- Layer thickness: $t$
- Road width: $W$
- Deposition velocity: $v_d$
- Wall (perimeter) count: $n_w$

### Step 1: Number of layers
Each layer is of thickness $t$, so the number of layers required to build height $H$ is:

$$N_{layers} = \frac{H}{t}$$

### Step 2: Total length of road per layer
Within each layer, the roads span the area $L \times B$ with spacing $W$. The number of parallel roads in one direction is:

$$N_{roads} = \frac{L}{W}$$

Each road has length $B$ (in the direction of travel). The total road length per layer is therefore:

$$L_{layer} = N_{roads} \times B + (\text{perimeters})$$

For a simplified estimate (ignoring perimeters):

$$L_{layer} \approx \frac{L \cdot B}{W}$$

Including infill factor $\rho_i$:

$$L_{layer,\,effective} = \rho_i \cdot \frac{L \cdot B}{W} + n_w \cdot 2(L+B)$$

### Step 3: Total build time
Time per layer = $\dfrac{L_{layer,\,effective}}{v_d}$

Therefore, total build time is:

$$T_{build} = \frac{H}{t} \cdot \frac{L_{layer,\,effective}}{v_d}$$

### Step 4: Substituting and simplifying (no perimeter case)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{build} &= \frac{H}{t} \cdot \frac{\rho_i \cdot L \cdot B}{W \cdot v_d} \\
&= \frac{\rho_i \cdot L \cdot B \cdot H}{t \cdot W \cdot v_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the numerator $\rho_i \cdot L \cdot B \cdot H$ is the **effective volume of plastic** used. The denominator $t \cdot W \cdot v_d$ is the **volumetric deposition rate**. This is a clean physical interpretation: time = volume ÷ rate.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Parameters on Part Quality

| Process Parameter ↑ (Increased) | Surface Finish | Build Speed | Strength (Z) | Strength (XY) | Warping Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer thickness (t)** | Worsens (stair-stepping) | Improves | Decreases | Slight decrease | Decreases |
| **Print speed (v)** | Worsens (vibrations) | Improves | Decreases (poor bonding) | Decreases | Increases |
| **Nozzle temp (Tn)** | Improves (better flow) | Slight improve | Improves (better fusion) | Improves | Increases (residual stress) |
| **Bed temp (Tb)** | — | — | Improves (first-layer adhesion) | — | **Decreases** |
| **Raster angle (θ)** | — | — | θ = 0° maximises XY strength | θ = 90° maximises Z strength | — |
| **Infill density (ρi)** | — | Worsens (more material) | Improves | Improves | — |
| **Cooling fan speed** | Improves (sharper features) | — | **Decreases** (premature solidification) | — | Decreases |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid for KTU viva:** "Thickness up, detail down. Speed up, strength down. Temp up, bond up. Raster aligned with load, strength up." This single sentence covers 80% of the examiner's likely follow-up questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic 1: FDM Process Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model] --> B[STL Tessellation]
    B --> C[Slicer Software]
    C --> D[G-code Generation]
    D --> E[Filament Spool]
    E --> F[Drive Gear Extruder]
    F --> G[Heated Liquefier Hot End]
    G --> H[Nozzle Tip]
    H --> I[Build Platform XY Motion]
    I --> J[Deposited Road Layer n]
    J --> K[Z Axis Drop by t]
    K --> L{All Layers Done}
    L -->|No| H
    L -->|Yes| M[Part with Supports]
    M --> N[Support Removal]
    N --> O[Finished FDM Part]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagram follows the safe-alphanumeric node ID rule (no reserved keywords) and uses clean uppercase labels without bold or HTML tags inside double quotes, per Mermaid safeguards.

## 4.2 Schematic 2: Detailed Nozzle & Liquefier Cross-Section (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Filament In d_f] --> B[Drive Gear]
    B --> C[Heater Block Tn]
    C --> D[Melting Zone]
    D --> E[Nozzle Orifice d_n]
    E --> F[Deposition on Build Platform]
    F --> G[Road Width W, Thickness t]
    G --> H[Layer Bonding to Previous Layer]
    H --> I[Cooling Fan Airflow]
    I --> J[Solidified Road]
```

```mermaid
    end

        C1[Diagonal roads for isotropic strength]
    end
```

```mermaid
        X4 --> X5[Heated Chamber Environment]
    end
    X5 --> Y1[Substrate on Rotary Table]
    Y1 --> Y2[In-Situ Monitoring IR Camera]
    Y2 --> Y3[Closed-Loop Process Control]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Format:** Short-answer type, 60–80 word answer expected, KTU Board style.

### **Q1. Define Fused Deposition Modelling. List any four commonly used thermoplastic materials in FDM.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) is a material-extrusion based additive manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is melted in a heated liquefier and extruded through a nozzle to build a part layer-by-layer, with each new layer fusing to the previous one upon cooling and solidification.

Four commonly used thermoplastics in FDM are:
1. **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** — biodegradable, easy to print.
2. **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** — tough, heat-resistant.
3. **PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol)** — chemical-resistant, ductile.
4. **Nylon (Polyamide)** — flexible, high strength.

### **Q2. What is the role of the build platform (heated bed) in FDM?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The heated build platform (typically held at 50 °C – 110 °C) keeps the bottom layers of the part warm, above the glass transition temperature of the polymer. This:
1. Improves **first-layer adhesion** and prevents the part from detaching mid-print.
2. Reduces **differential cooling**, which mitigates **warping** and residual stress.
3. Promotes uniform **thermal contraction** across the part, improving dimensional accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is split into sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. A genuine internal choice is required.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**Q(a). With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of the Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) process. List the major components of an FDM system. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle of FDM:**
The FDM process builds a part layer-by-layer through the controlled extrusion of a thermoplastic filament. The workflow is:

1. A 3D CAD model is converted to STL and sliced into horizontal layers of thickness *t* by a slicer.
2. The G-code is sent to the printer's controller, which actuates stepper motors driving the X, Y, and Z axes.
3. A drive gear (extruder) pulls filament from the spool into the **liquefier (hot-end)**, where it is heated to a semi-molten state (above Tg, near Tm).
4. The molten polymer is forced through a fine **nozzle** (typical diameter 0.2 – 0.8 mm), depositing a thin **road** of width *W* and height *t* onto the build platform along the prescribed toolpath.
5. After each layer is completed, the **build platform descends by one layer thickness**, and the next layer is deposited on top, fusing through thermal welding.
6. The process repeats until the full part is formed, after which **support structures** (if any) are removed.

**Major Components of an FDM System:**

1. **Filament spool and feeding mechanism** — supplies raw thermoplastic.
2. **Drive gear / extruder** — pulls the filament.
3. **Heated liquefier (hot-end)** — melts the polymer.
4. **Nozzle** — controls extrudate size and shape.
5. **Build platform (often heated)** — supports the part.
6. **XYZ motion system (stepper motors + lead screws / belts)** — positions the head.
7. **Enclosure and part-cooling fan** — manages thermal environment.
8. **Controller + firmware** — interprets G-code and drives the system.

**Q(b). A desktop FDM printer is to print a part of bounding dimensions 100 mm × 80 mm × 40 mm (L × B × H) with 30 % infill density, layer thickness 0.2 mm, road width 0.4 mm, and print speed 60 mm/s. Estimate the build time (ignore perimeters, support, and travel moves). [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Number of layers**
$$N_{layers} = \frac{H}{t} = \frac{40}{0.2} = 200 \text{ layers}$$

**Step 2: Effective road length per layer**
$$L_{layer} = \rho_i \cdot \frac{L \cdot B}{W} = 0.30 \times \frac{100 \times 80}{0.4} = 0.30 \times 20000 = 6000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Time per layer**
$$t_{layer} = \frac{L_{layer}}{v_d} = \frac{6000}{60} = 100 \text{ s/layer}$$

**Step 4: Total build time**
$$T_{build} = N_{layers} \times t_{layer} = 200 \times 100 = 20000 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{build} = 20000 \text{ s} \approx 5.56 \text{ hours}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**Q(a). Explain the major process parameters of FDM and discuss their influence on the mechanical properties of the printed part. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The major FDM process parameters and their influence are:

1. **Layer Thickness (t):** Thinner layers give finer resolution and stronger inter-layer bonding (larger contact area per unit volume), improving tensile and flexural strength. Thicker layers accelerate build time but reduce mechanical performance.

2. **Raster Angle (θ):** The orientation of roads within a layer directly determines the anisotropic strength of the part. Aligned raster (0°) maximises strength along the road direction, while 90° raster maximises strength across roads. ±45° gives quasi-isotropic behaviour. The Z-direction (inter-layer) strength is **always the weakest**, typically 50–80 % of XY strength.

3. **Infill Density (ρi):** Higher infill means more material per unit volume, increasing tensile, compressive, and flexural strength — but at the cost of more material and longer build time.

4. **Nozzle Temperature (Tn):** Higher temperatures reduce melt viscosity, enabling better inter-road and inter-layer diffusion, thus improving bonding strength. Excessively high temperatures cause stringing and degradation.

5. **Print Speed (v_d):** Faster printing reduces the time available for thermal diffusion between roads, weakening the bond. Slower speeds improve strength but extend build time.

6. **Build Orientation:** Parts printed flat (XY plane) are far stronger than those printed vertically (Z direction), due to the inherent weakness of inter-layer bonding.

**Q(b). Two thermoplastic materials — ABS (Density 1.04 g/cm³) and PLA (Density 1.25 g/cm³) — are considered for an FDM part. The filament is 1.75 mm in diameter, fed at 4 mm/s. The nozzle radius is 0.2 mm, road width 0.4 mm, and layer thickness 0.15 mm. Calculate the mass of plastic extruded per minute, and identify which material gives a higher mass deposition rate. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Volumetric flow rate of filament**
$$Q_{in} = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi (1.75)^2}{4} \times 4 = 9.621 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Convert units**
$$Q_{in} = 9.621 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} = 9.621 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = 0.5773 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Mass flow rate for ABS**
$$\dot{m}_{ABS} = Q \cdot \rho_{ABS} = 0.5773 \times 1.04 = 0.6004 \text{ g/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{ABS} = 0.6004 \times 60 = 36.02 \text{ g/min}$$

**Step 4: Mass flow rate for PLA**
$$\dot{m}_{PLA} = Q \cdot \rho_{PLA} = 0.5773 \times 1.25 = 0.7216 \text{ g/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{PLA} = 0.7216 \times 60 = 43.30 \text{ g/min}$$

**Step 5: Comparison**

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{PLA} = 43.30 \text{ g/min} > \dot{m}_{ABS} = 36.02 \text{ g/min}}$$

Since the volumetric flow rate is the same for both (driven by the same filament feed and nozzle), the material with higher density (PLA) gives a higher mass deposition rate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in FDM Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always state mm³/s, g/min, MPa, etc. A correct number with no unit may fetch 0 marks in the final step.
> 2. **Confusing *W* and *d_n*:** Road width is *not* always equal to nozzle diameter — it depends on the **swell ratio** and **layer thickness**. If the question gives a specific *W*, use it; do not assume $W = d_n$.
> 3. **Sign error in build time:** Time per layer must be summed (not multiplied) across layers. A frequent slip is $T = N/t$ instead of $T = N \times t_{layer}$.
> 4. **Ignoring infill factor:** Students often compute the build time for a 100 % solid part when only 30 % infill is asked. This leads to a 3× overestimate.
> 5. **Mixing up raster and build orientation:** Raster angle = orientation of roads *within* a layer. Build orientation = orientation of the part *on the platform*. These are two different concepts.
> 6. **No diagram in Q(a):** For 7-mark process explanation questions, a labelled schematic is **mandatory**. Expect to lose 1–2 marks without it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this section for the night before the exam!)**

- ☐ FDM = Material Extrusion (ASTM F2792) → invented by **Scott Crump, 1989**, commercialised by **Stratasys**.
- ☐ Filament diameter standard: **1.75 mm or 2.85 mm**.
- ☐ Nozzle temperature: **180 °C – 260 °C**; bed: **50 °C – 110 °C**.
- ☐ Layer thickness: **0.05 – 0.3 mm**; road width: **0.4 – 0.8 mm**.
- ☐ **Master Equation:** $Q = v_d \cdot W \cdot t = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$
- ☐ Build time (no perimeters): $T_{build} = \dfrac{\rho_i \cdot L \cdot B \cdot H}{t \cdot W \cdot v_d}$
- ☐ **Z-direction is the weakest direction** in FDM parts (inter-layer bond).
- ☐ Raster angle is the biggest lever for **mechanical anisotropy**.
- ☐ Support structures are required for overhangs beyond **45°** from vertical.
- ☐ Common materials: **PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon, TPU, PEEK** (high-end).
- ☐ Soluble supports: **PVA (for PLA), HIPS (for ABS, dissolved in limonene)**.
- ☐ Surface roughness side-walls: $R_a \approx 0.5 \cdot t$.
- ☐ **Volumetric flow rate** is the single most important controllable process variable.
- ☐ **FDM** is a registered trademark of Stratasys; open-source community uses **FFF** (Fused Filament Fabrication).
- ☐ Energy consumption: typically 0.05 – 0.2 kWh per kg of printed part.
- ☐ Common defects: **warping, delamination, stringing, under-/over-extrusion, elephant's foot, ringing/ghosting**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle, Materials, Process Parameters, Advantages and Applications of Various AM Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET616 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle, Materials, Process Parameters, Advantages and Applications of Various AM Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** *Principle, Materials, Process Parameters, Advantages and Applications of Various AM Processes* — covering the **seven ISO/ASTM 52900 families** of additive manufacturing.

### 1.1 What is an "AM Process Family"?

An **AM process family** is a grouping of additive manufacturing technologies that share the **same fundamental physical principle of consolidation**. ISO/ASTM 52900 (the international standard for AM terminology) classifies *every* AM technology into exactly **seven process families**. The classification is **principle-based**, not machine-brand based.

**The Seven ISO/ASTM 52900 Families:**

| # | Process Family | Acronym |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vat Photopolymerization | **VP** |
| 2 | Material Extrusion | **ME** |
| 3 | Material Jetting | **MJ** |
| 4 | Binder Jetting | **BJ** |
| 5 | Powder Bed Fusion | **PBF** |
| 6 | Directed Energy Deposition | **DED** |
| 7 | Sheet Lamination | **SL** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Seven Cooking Methods" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a chef who can prepare potatoes in **seven different ways** — boiling, frying, baking, grilling, roasting, mashing, and shredding. Every time the chef changes the *cooking principle*, the equipment, ingredients, and the resulting taste/texture change. Similarly, in AM, when the engineer changes the **physical principle** of consolidation (light-curing, melting with a laser, jetting droplets, jetting a binder, rolling sheets, etc.), the materials, machines, accuracy, and applications all change. The seven process families are the chef's seven "cooking methods" for building parts layer by layer.

### 1.3 Formal Definition of the Seven Process Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VP (Vat Photopolymerization):** An AM process in which a **liquid photopolymer resin** in a vat is selectively **cured and solidified** layer-by-layer by **actinic radiation** (typically UV laser or projected UV light).
>
> **ME (Material Extrusion):** An AM process in which a **filament, pellets, or paste** is selectively dispensed through a **nozzle or orifice** and solidified by a thermal or chemical phase change.
>
> **MJ (Material Jetting):** An AM process in which **droplets of build material** are selectively jetted (inkjet-like) and **cured/solidified** to form a layer.
>
> **BJ (Binder Jetting):** An AM process in which a **liquid bonding agent** is selectively deposited onto a bed of **powder particles** to bond them together layer by layer.
>
> **PBF (Powder Bed Fusion):** An AM process in which **thermal energy** (laser or electron beam) selectively fuses regions of a **powder bed** to form a solid layer.
>
> **DED (Directed Energy Deposition):** An AM process in which **focused thermal energy** is used to **melt material as it is being deposited** (typically through a nozzle) onto a substrate.
>
> **SL (Sheet Lamination):** An AM process in which **sheets of material** are bonded together layer-by-layer and then cut to the required shape (mechanically or by laser).

### 1.4 Why Classify AM Processes?

> [!NOTE]
> - To **standardize terminology** across industries, research papers, and procurement contracts.
> - To help **designers select the right process** for a given functional requirement (e.g., biocompatibility → MJ; metal part → PBF/DED; large sand mould → BJ).
> - To enable **quality and certification standards** (e.g., ASTM F42, ISO/TC 261).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Process-by-Process Breakdown

---

#### **A. VAT PHOTOPOLYMERIZATION (VP) — Stereolithography (SLA) & Digital Light Processing (DLP)**

**Principle:**
- A vat of **liquid photopolymer resin** is exposed to controlled light.
- **SLA** uses a **UV laser** to trace each cross-section; the laser is steered by **galvanometer mirrors** (X-Y scanning).
- **DLP** uses a **digital micromirror device (DMD)** to project an entire 2D mask of UV light at once — curing a full layer in a single flash.
- Photoinitiators in the resin absorb photons, generate free radicals, and trigger **chain-growth polymerization**, converting liquid monomers/oligomers into a crosslinked solid network.
- After each layer cures, the build platform moves (either **up** — top-down, or **down** — bottom-up), a recoater blade re-spreads fresh resin, and the next layer is cured.

**Materials:**
- **Photopolymer resins:**
  - Standard (acrylic/epoxy-based) — for prototypes.
  - Tough/resilient (ABS-like, polypropylene-like).
  - Castable (for lost-wax investment casting patterns).
  - Biocompatible/dental (ISO 10993).
  - Clear/transparent (for optics and flow visualization).
  - High-temperature (HDT > 200 °C).
  - Ceramic-filled and metal-filled (for sacrificial moulds).

**Process Parameters:**
- **Layer thickness (h):** typically **25 µm – 100 µm** (finer = smoother, slower).
- **Laser power (P):** typically **100 mW – 1 W**.
- **Scan speed (v):** typically **1 – 10 m/s**.
- **Hatch spacing (d):** typically **0.05 – 0.15 mm**.
- **Critical exposure (E_c):** the minimum energy density at which the resin gels.
- **Penetration depth (D_p):** the depth at which the light intensity falls to 1/e of the surface value.
- **Build orientation:** drastically affects mechanical strength, surface finish, and support requirements.
- **Post-curing:** required for full polymerization (typically 30–60 min in a UV oven).

**Advantages:**
- **Highest accuracy and surface finish** of all AM processes (down to ±25 µm).
- Smooth surface — minimal post-processing.
- Excellent for intricate details, thin walls, and clear parts.
- Wide range of functional resins (biocompatible, tough, high-temp).

**Applications:**
- **Dental & medical:** surgical guides, aligners, crowns, anatomical models.
- **Jewellery:** investment casting patterns.
- **Prototyping:** concept models with high cosmetic finish.
- **Microfluidics:** clear, optically transparent lab-on-chip devices.
- **Optical components:** lenses, light guides.

---

#### **B. MATERIAL EXTRUSION (ME) — Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) / Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)**

**Principle:**
- A **thermoplastic filament** (typically 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm diameter) is fed into a **heated liquefier/nozzle**.
- The molten polymer is extruded and deposited along a toolpath, where it **solidifies through cooling**.
- The build platform moves in the **X–Y plane**, while the Z axis is incremented after each layer.

**Materials:**
- **Standard polymers:** PLA (Polylactic Acid), ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene).
- **Engineering polymers:** PETG, ASA, PC (Polycarbonate), Nylon (PA12, PA6).
- **High-performance polymers:** PEEK, PEKK, ULTEM (PEI).
- **Composites:** carbon-fibre-reinforced nylon, glass-fibre-filled, wood-filled, metal-filled (decorative).
- **Elastomers:** TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane).
- **Soluble support:** PVA (Polyvinyl Alcohol), HIPS (High Impact Polystyrene).
- **Wax and pastes** (for ceramic/FDM-printing of metal feedstocks).

**Process Parameters:**
- **Nozzle temperature (T_n):** typically **180 °C – 400 °C** (PLA ≈ 200 °C; PEEK ≈ 400 °C).
- **Build plate temperature (T_b):** typically **25 °C – 150 °C**.
- **Layer thickness (h):** typically **0.1 – 0.4 mm**.
- **Print speed (v):** typically **30 – 150 mm/s**.
- **Nozzle diameter (d):** typically **0.2 – 0.8 mm**.
- **Infill density (%):** **0 % – 100 %**; pattern (line, grid, gyroid, honeycomb).
- **Raster angle (θ):** typically **0°/90°** alternating layers.
- **Chamber temperature:** for high-temp polymers (e.g., PEEK) — up to **90 °C – 120 °C**.

**Advantages:**
- **Lowest cost** of all AM processes — both machine and material.
- Wide material selection including commodity and high-performance polymers.
- Simple to operate, suitable for **office/desktop** use.
- Multiple materials (dissolvable support).
- Good mechanical strength along layer direction.

**Applications:**
- **Concept prototypes**, jigs, fixtures.
- **End-use parts:** drone frames, brackets, enclosures.
- **Medical implants** (PEEK cranial implants).
- **Architectural models.**
- **Educational projects, hobbyist, DIY.**
- **Investment casting patterns** (using wax or castable filaments).

---

#### **C. MATERIAL JETTING (MJ) — PolyJet, MultiJet Modeling (MJM), Nano Particle Jetting (NPJ)**

**Principle:**
- **Inkjet print heads** (thermal or piezoelectric) deposit tiny droplets (picoliters) of **photopolymer or wax** material.
- Each droplet is **immediately cured by UV light**.
- Multiple print heads allow **simultaneous deposition of multiple materials** and a soluble support material.
- The build platform lowers after each layer; the process repeats.

**Materials:**
- **Photopolymer resins** (similar to VP) — rigid, flexible, transparent, high-temperature, biocompatible.
- **Wax** (for casting).
- **Metal nanoparticle suspensions** (for NPJ — produces metal parts after sintering).
- **Multiple materials in a single build** — rigid + flexible in any desired ratio.

**Process Parameters:**
- **Drop volume:** typically **1 – 80 picoliters**.
- **Printhead resolution:** typically **300 – 1200 DPI**.
- **Layer thickness:** typically **14 – 32 µm** — extremely fine.
- **Curing energy:** **UV intensity and time** per droplet.
- **Material combinations:** digital blending in 0.1% increments.

**Advantages:**
- **Multi-material, multi-colour** capability in a single build.
- **Excellent surface finish** (comparable to VP).
- **High accuracy** (±20 µm).
- Digital material blending for **gradient properties** (e.g., shore hardness A27 → D85 in one print).
- No support marks where soluble support is used.

**Applications:**
- **Realistic anatomical models** with multi-coloured tissues (hearts, kidneys, skulls) for surgical planning.
- **Moulds with conformal cooling** inserts.
- **Injection moulding prototypes** with overmoulded rubber-like seals.
- **Jewellery prototyping** with realistic appearance.
- **Optical prototypes** with varying translucency.

---

#### **D. BINDER JETTING (BJ)**

**Principle:**
- A thin layer of **powder** (metal, sand, ceramic, or polymer) is spread across the build platform.
- A **print head** deposits droplets of a **liquid binder** selectively onto the powder bed.
- The binder bonds adjacent powder particles — locally forming a cross-section.
- After all layers are printed, the part is in a **"green" state** and must be **depowdered**, then **post-processed** (cured, sintered, or infiltrated).

**Materials:**
- **Metal powders:** stainless steel 316L, bronze, titanium, Inconel 625, cobalt-chrome.
- **Sand (silica or zircon).**
- **Ceramics:** alumina, zirconia, silica.
- **Polymer powders:** PMMA, polystyrene.
- **Binders:** water-based, solvent-based, or polymeric resins.

**Process Parameters:**
- **Layer thickness:** typically **0.08 – 0.2 mm**.
- **Binder saturation (%):** typically **50 % – 200 %**.
- **Drop volume:** **30 – 80 pL**.
- **Printhead resolution:** typically **300 – 600 DPI**.
- **Powder size distribution:** typically **D50 ≈ 30 – 50 µm**.
- **Post-processing:**
  - **Sintering** (metal/ceramic): furnace at high temperature for densification.
  - **Infiltration** (bronze/steel): infiltrate with bronze or copper to fill pores.

**Advantages:**
- **No support structures** required — powder bed acts as self-support.
- **Large build volumes** possible.
- **High build speed** — print head does not need to scan each contour with energy.
- **Multi-material / multi-colour** capability (different binders or coloured powders).
- Low thermal residual stresses.

**Applications:**
- **Large sand moulds and cores** for metal casting (foundry).
- **Metal parts** with infiltration (decorative and functional).
- **Investment casting shell patterns** (e.g., Voxeljet — large-size).
- **Architectural models.**
- **Functional metal parts** for low-volume production.

---

#### **E. POWDER BED FUSION (PBF) — Includes SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM**

**Principle:**
- A thin layer of **powder** is spread by a recoater blade or roller across the build platform.
- An **energy source** (laser or electron beam) selectively scans the powder bed, **fusing particles** by either:
  - **Sintering** (below melting point) — SLS.
  - **Full melting** — SLM (Selective Laser Melting) / DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering).
  - **Melting under electron beam** — EBM.
- After scanning, the platform lowers by one layer thickness; a new layer of powder is spread; and the cycle repeats.
- Parts are **embedded in unfused powder** at end of build.

**Sub-families and Materials:**

| Sub-process | Energy Source | Typical Materials |
|---|---|---|
| **SLS** (Selective Laser Sintering) | CO₂ laser | Polyamide (PA12/PA11), TPU, polystyrene, PEEK |
| **DMLS / SLM** | Yb-fibre laser | Stainless steel 316L/17-4PH, AlSi10Mg, Ti6Al4V, Inconel 718/625, CoCrMo |
| **EBM** (Electron Beam Melting) | Electron beam (vacuum) | Ti6Al4V (mainly), CoCrMo, Inconel 718 |

**Process Parameters:**
- **Laser power (P):** typically **50 – 1000 W**.
- **Scan speed (v):** typically **200 – 2000 mm/s**.
- **Hatch spacing (h):** typically **0.08 – 0.15 mm**.
- **Layer thickness (t):** typically **20 – 80 µm**.
- **Oxygen content** (must be < 1000 ppm for metal PBF).
- **Build plate temperature (T_b):** for metal ≈ **80 – 200 °C**; for SLS polymer ≈ **170 – 180 °C**.
- **Scan strategy:** island/hatch/contour/chequerboard.
- **Volumetric Energy Density (VED):** see formula sheet.

**Advantages:**
- **Excellent mechanical properties** (often comparable to wrought metal).
- **High complexity** with internal channels (lattice, conformal cooling).
- **No support for overhangs** in SLS — powder bed acts as self-support.
- For SLM/DMLS/EBM, **biocompatible metals** (Ti6Al4V, CoCrMo) for implants.

**Applications:**
- **Aerospace:** topology-optimised brackets, fuel nozzles, heat exchangers.
- **Medical:** patient-specific orthopaedic and dental implants (cranial, hip, spinal).
- **Automotive:** functional prototypes, jigs, low-volume end-use parts.
- **Tooling:** conformal-cooled injection mould inserts.
- **Jewellery:** direct metal patterns.

---

#### **F. DIRECTED ENERGY DEPOSITION (DED) — LMD, LENS, EBF³, Plasma Arc Additive Manufacturing (PAAM), WAAM**

**Principle:**
- **Material (powder or wire)** is fed through a **multi-axis nozzle**.
- Simultaneously, a **focused energy source** (laser, electron beam, plasma arc, or MIG/TIG arc) creates a **molten pool** on the substrate.
- The molten pool is deposited along a toolpath — building up **layer by layer in 3D**.
- The substrate or nozzle moves in **multiple axes (typically 4–5 axes)** — enabling repair and feature addition on existing parts.

**Sub-families and Materials:**

| Sub-process | Energy | Feedstock | Materials |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LMD / LENS** | Laser | Powder | Ti6Al4V, Inconel 718, Stellite, WC-Co, H13 |
| **EBF³** | Electron beam | Wire | Ti6Al4V |
| **WAAM** | TIG / MIG / Plasma | Wire | Mild steel, stainless steel, Ti, Al, Inconel |

**Process Parameters:**
- **Laser power (P):** typically **500 W – 10 kW**.
- **Powder flow rate (ṁ):** typically **1 – 25 g/min**.
- **Travel speed (v):** typically **5 – 25 mm/s**.
- **Layer thickness:** typically **0.5 – 5 mm** (much thicker than PBF).
- **Nozzle stand-off distance:** typically **5 – 15 mm**.
- **Shielding gas flow:** typically **Ar** for laser/electron beam; **Ar/He** for plasma.

**Advantages:**
- **High deposition rate** (kg/hour) — suitable for **large parts**.
- **Multi-axis deposition** — can **repair** damaged components (e.g., turbine blades).
- **Functionally graded materials** — change composition during deposition.
- **Hybrid manufacturing** — combine with CNC machining in same platform.
- Works on **existing substrates** without a powder bed.

**Applications:**
- **Aerospace:** large Ti structural components, turbine blade repair.
- **Marine/offshore:** propeller and shaft repair.
- **Oil & gas:** corrosion-resistant overlays (Inconel).
- **Heavy industry:** large steel structural components (WAAM).
- **Biomedical:** custom implants (rare vs. PBF).

---

#### **G. SHEET LAMINATION (SL) — Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM), Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)**

**Principle:**
- **LOM:** Sheets of **paper, polymer, or metal foil** (adhesive-backed) are successively **rolled onto a build platform**, and a **laser or knife cuts** the cross-section. Unbound areas remain as waste and are later **decubed** (removed).
- **UAM:** Metal foil layers are bonded via **ultasonic welding**, with periodic **CNC milling** for contouring.

**Materials:**
- **LOM:** paper, plastic film, adhesive-backed metal foil (Al, Cu, stainless).
- **UAM:** aluminium, copper, titanium, stainless steel foil.

**Process Parameters (LOM):**
- **Sheet thickness:** typically **0.05 – 0.2 mm**.
- **Laser cutting power / speed.**
- **Roller pressure and temperature.**
- **Adhesive type and activation.**

**Advantages:**
- **Low cost** for LOM (paper-based prototyping).
- **UAM** can produce **multi-metal composites** and **embedded sensors** (because bonding is solid-state, low temperature).
- **Large parts** possible.
- Minimal residual stress (especially UAM).

**Applications:**
- **LOM:** large visual prototypes, casting patterns, sand-cored moulds (via paper laminates).
- **UAM:** smart structures with embedded electronics, heat exchangers with internal cooling channels, dissimilar metal parts.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Process Window Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **most frequently asked** numerical/numerical-conceptual problems in KTU ESE for AM.

**Formulas:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric Energy Density (VED) | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | P = laser power, v = scan speed, h = hatch spacing, t = layer thickness | $\text{J/mm}^3$ |
| 2 | Areal Energy Density (AED) | $E_a = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | P = laser power, v = scan speed, h = hatch spacing | $\text{J/mm}^2$ |
| 3 | Linear Energy Density (LED) | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | P = laser power, v = scan speed | $\text{J/mm}$ |
| 4 | Cure Depth (Beer-Lambert, VP/SLA) | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_{\max}}{E_c}\right)$ | D_p = penetration depth, E_max = max exposure, E_c = critical exposure | mm |
| 5 | Beam Diameter & Resolution | $d \approx \dfrac{2 \cdot \lambda \cdot f}{\pi \cdot w_0}$ | λ = laser wavelength, f = focal length, w_0 = beam waist | mm |
| 6 | Volumetric Shrinkage Compensation | $\Delta V = V_0 \cdot S_v$ | V_0 = original volume, S_v = volumetric shrinkage fraction | mm³ |
| 7 | Build Time Estimate | $T_b = \dfrac{N_l \cdot A_l}{v} + t_s \cdot N_l$ | N_l = number of layers, A_l = area scanned per layer, v = scan speed, t_s = settling time per layer | seconds |
| 8 | Powder Recoating Time | $t_r = \dfrac{L_b}{v_r}$ | L_b = bed length, v_r = recoater velocity | s |
| 9 | Staircase Error (Step Error) | $\delta_s = h \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | h = layer thickness, θ = surface angle from vertical | mm |
| 10 | Mass of Material (extrusion) | $m = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v \cdot t$ | ρ = density, d = nozzle diameter, v = extrusion velocity, t = time | g |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **VED** is the **#1 process window metric** for SLM/DMLS/EBM. If $E_v$ is too low → lack of fusion porosity. Too high → keyhole porosity, evaporation, residual stress.
> - **Cure depth** decides whether a VP layer fully polymerizes through — important for **over-curing** between layers.
> - **Staircase error** drives the choice of **layer thickness** and **part orientation** in any AM process.
> - The formulas apply directly to **design rules** in any KTU design problem on "selecting an AM process for a given application".

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

**Starting Principle:**
The energy delivered by a moving laser is **power × time per unit volume** of material being consolidated.

**Step 1 — Energy delivered per unit length of scan:**
The energy in joules deposited in a unit length (mm) of scan is:
$$
E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}
$$
where P is in watts (J/s) and v is in mm/s. The units work out as $\text{J/mm}$.

**Step 2 — Energy delivered per unit area of one layer:**
For a single scan, the width of the melt track equals the hatch spacing $h$. Therefore the energy per unit area is:
$$
E_a = \dfrac{E_l}{h} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}
$$
Units: $\text{J/mm}^2$.

**Step 3 — Energy delivered per unit volume of one layer:**
A single layer has thickness $t$. Hence the volume per unit area is $1 \times t$, and the energy per unit volume is:
$$
E_v = \dfrac{E_a}{t} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$
Units: $\text{J/mm}^3$.

**Final Result:**
$$
E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation is the **canonical KTU answer** whenever the question asks "Define and derive volumetric energy density" or "Find VED given power, scan speed, hatch and layer thickness".

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem (KTU Model):**
In a Selective Laser Melting (SLM) process for Ti6Al4V, the process parameters are:
$P = 200\,\text{W},\ v = 800\,\text{mm/s},\ h = 0.1\,\text{mm},\ t = 30\,\mu\text{m}$.

Compute the Volumetric Energy Density (VED).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert all units to consistent SI-compatible form.
- $P = 200\,\text{W} = 200\,\text{J/s}$
- $v = 800\,\text{mm/s}$
- $h = 0.1\,\text{mm}$
- $t = 30\,\mu\text{m} = 0.03\,\text{mm}$

Step 2 — Substitute into VED formula:
$$
E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \dfrac{200}{800 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 0.03}
$$

Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator:
$$
800 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 0.03 = 2.4
$$

Step 4 — Final computation:
$$
E_v = \dfrac{200}{2.4} = 83.33\,\text{J/mm}^3
$$

Step 5 — Sanity check against literature:
The reported optimum VED for SLM of Ti6Al4V is **60 – 100 J/mm³**. The computed value falls within the optimal window. The part is likely to be near-full density. ✔

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert µm to mm and report a value **1000× off**. Always write the unit conversion step explicitly.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Cure Depth in Vat Photopolymerization (Beer-Lambert Model)

**Starting Principle:**
The intensity of UV light inside the resin falls off **exponentially** with depth — described by the Beer-Lambert law:
$$
I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}
$$
where $I_0$ is the surface intensity, $z$ is depth, and $D_p$ is the penetration depth.

**Step 1 — Define exposure:**
The total exposure (energy per unit area) at depth z is:
$$
E(z) = I(z) \cdot \Delta t = E_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the cure criterion:**
Polymerization (gelation) occurs when exposure exceeds the **critical exposure** $E_c$:
$$
E(z_c) = E_c \implies E_0 \cdot e^{-z_c/D_p} = E_c
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the cure depth $z_c = C_d$:**
$$
e^{-C_d/D_p} = \dfrac{E_c}{E_0}
$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:
$$
-\dfrac{C_d}{D_p} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_c}{E_0}\right) = -\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Final formula:**
$$
\boxed{C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $E_0$ and $E_c$ must be in the **same units** (typically mJ/cm²). $D_p$ and $C_d$ are both in mm.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Cure Depth)

**Problem:**
In an SLA process, $D_p = 0.15\,\text{mm}$, $E_0 = 25\,\text{mJ/cm}^2$, $E_c = 8\,\text{mJ/cm}^2$. Find the cure depth.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Substitute into the formula:
$$
C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)
$$

Step 2 — Compute the ratio:
$$
\dfrac{E_0}{E_c} = \dfrac{25}{8} = 3.125
$$

Step 3 — Take the natural log:
$$
\ln(3.125) \approx 1.1394
$$

Step 4 — Multiply by penetration depth:
$$
C_d = 0.15 \cdot 1.1394 \approx 0.171\,\text{mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means the laser cures the resin to a depth of **≈ 171 µm** in a single exposure — useful for choosing the layer thickness (commonly set at ~50% of $C_d$ for safety).

---

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix — All Seven AM Process Families

| Parameter | VP (SLA/DLP) | ME (FDM) | MJ (PolyJet) | BJ | PBF (SLM) | DED (LMD) | SL (LOM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | ±25–100 µm | ±100–200 µm | ±20–50 µm | ±100–200 µm | ±50–200 µm | ±250–500 µm | ±150–300 µm |
| **Surface roughness (Ra)** | 1–5 µm | 10–25 µm | 2–10 µm | 8–20 µm | 5–20 µm | 10–30 µm | 10–25 µm |
| **Min. layer thickness** | 25 µm | 50 µm | 14 µm | 80 µm | 20 µm | 200 µm | 50 µm |
| **Typical build speed** | Slow | Medium | Medium | Fast | Slow | Very fast | Medium |
| **Material types** | Polymers, ceramics | Thermoplastics, composites | Polymers, wax | Metals, sand, ceramics | Metals, polymers | Metals (powder/wire) | Paper, polymer, metal foil |
| **Support required?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes (metal) / No (SLS) | No (usually) | No |
| **Post-processing** | UV post-cure | Minimal | UV post-cure | Depowder + sinter/infiltrate | HIP, support removal, machining | Machining, HIP | Decubing, machining |
| **Cost of machine** | Medium–High | Low | High | Medium–High | Very High | Very High | Low–Medium |
| **Typical applications** | Dental, jewellery, microfluidics | Prototypes, low-volume parts | Multi-material prototypes | Sand moulds, large metal parts | Aerospace, medical implants | Large metal, repair | Visual prototypes, paper moulds |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C -- Yes --> D{Parts in single material?}

    K -- Yes --> L[DED / LMD / WAAM]
    K -- No --> M[Evaluate BJ + Sinter]
    H -- Large --> N[Is mechanical strength critical?]
    N -- Yes --> O[WAAM / DED]
    N -- No --> P[BJ Sand Mould]

    B -- Multi-material or full colour --> R[MJ / PolyJet]
    E --> S[End]
    F --> S
    G --> S
    J --> S
    L --> S
    M --> S
    O --> S
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

```mermaid
    F --> G[Build Platform LOWER by t]

```


```mermaid
flowchart LR

    C -.After all layers.-> G[Green Part]
    G --> H[Solvent cleaning]

    I --> J[Support removal]
    J --> K[Finished SLA Part]
```
