## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 model]**
*List any three ISO/ASTM 52900 AM process families. Differentiate between Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) and Directed Energy Deposition (DED).*

**Model Answer:**

The seven ISO/ASTM 52900 AM process families are:
1. Vat Photopolymerization (VP)
2. Material Extrusion (ME)
3. Material Jetting (MJ)
4. Binder Jetting (BJ)
5. Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)
6. Directed Energy Deposition (DED)
7. Sheet Lamination (SL)

*(Any three of the above)*

**PBF vs. DED:**

| Aspect | PBF | DED |
|---|---|---|
| Powder bed | Required | Not used (powder fed through nozzle) |
| Energy source | Laser or electron beam | Laser, electron beam, plasma, or arc |
| Build envelope | Limited by powder bed size | Large — multi-axis |
| Resolution | High (thin layers) | Lower (thick layers, mm-scale) |
| Support | Powder bed acts as self-support (SLS) or solid support (SLM) | No support needed for most builds |
| Primary use | Fine, complex parts; implants; aerospace brackets | Large parts, repair, feature addition, gradient materials |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 model]**
*State the principle of Stereolithography (SLA). Mention two commonly used photopolymer resins and their applications.*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
SLA is a Vat Photopolymerization (VP) process in which a vat of **liquid photopolymer resin** is selectively cured layer-by-layer using a **UV laser**. The laser traces each cross-section, and **photoinitiators** in the resin absorb the UV photons to start a **chain-growth free-radical (or cationic) polymerization**. Each cured layer is lowered (or raised) by the layer thickness $h$, fresh resin is recoated, and the cycle repeats until the part is complete.

**Resins and applications:**
1. **Tough/ABS-like resin** — used for **functional prototypes** that require impact resistance (e.g., snap-fit enclosures, brackets).
2. **Castable resin** — used for **investment casting patterns** in jewellery (the resin burns out cleanly, leaving a hollow mould).

*(Other acceptable resins: biocompatible/dental resin — for surgical guides and crowns; high-temperature resin — for tooling and testing under thermal load; clear/transparent resin — for microfluidics and optics.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 model]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **principle of Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)**. List the **process parameters** that influence the quality of FDM parts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** process is used to print Ti6Al4V with parameters: $P = 250\,\text{W}$, $v = 1000\,\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.12\,\text{mm}$, $t = 30\,\mu\text{m}$. Compute the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** and comment on whether the part is expected to be near-full density. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Principle of FDM:**
- FDM is a **Material Extrusion (ME)** AM process.
- A **thermoplastic filament** (typically 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm diameter) is fed by an **extruder motor and drive gear** into a **heated liquefier** (temperature ≈ 200–400 °C depending on the material).
- The molten polymer is extruded through a **nozzle** (0.2–0.8 mm diameter) along a programmed **toolpath**, depositing a thin bead called a **road** or **raster**.
- The bead **solidifies rapidly** upon cooling and bonds to the previous layer.
- The build platform moves in the **X–Y plane** to form each layer, and the **Z-axis** is incremented by the layer thickness after each layer is completed.

```
    [Filament Spool] → [Extruder] → [Heated Nozzle] → [Bead deposition on build plate]
                                                                ↑ Z moves up each layer
```

**Process Parameters Influencing FDM Quality:**
1. **Nozzle temperature (T_n)** — too low → poor bonding; too high → stringing, oozing.
2. **Build plate temperature (T_b)** — affects first-layer adhesion and warping.
3. **Layer thickness (h)** — finer layers give better finish, slower build.
4. **Print speed (v)** — affects surface quality and dimensional accuracy.
5. **Infill density and pattern** — determines internal strength and material use.
6. **Raster angle (θ)** — controls anisotropic mechanical properties.
7. **Chamber temperature** — critical for high-temperature polymers (e.g., PEEK).
8. **Cooling fan speed** — controls crystallinity and warping.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters and units:**
- $P = 250\,\text{W}$
- $v = 1000\,\text{mm/s}$
- $h = 0.12\,\text{mm}$
- $t = 30\,\mu\text{m} = 0.03\,\text{mm}$

**Step 2 — Apply VED formula:**
$$
E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate denominator:**
$$
v \cdot h \cdot t = 1000 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 0.03 = 3.6
$$

**Step 4 — Final computation:**
$$
E_v = \dfrac{250}{3.6} \approx 69.44\,\text{J/mm}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
The literature-reported optimum VED for SLM of Ti6Al4V is **60–100 J/mm³**. The computed value of **69.44 J/mm³** lies comfortably in this range. The part is expected to be **near-full density (> 99.5 %)** with minimal lack-of-fusion porosity.

---

#### **QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 model]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, materials, advantages and applications** of **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)**. Compare SLS with SLA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an **SLA** process, the **penetration depth** of the resin is $D_p = 0.18\,\text{mm}$. The **maximum exposure** at the surface is $E_0 = 30\,\text{mJ/cm}^2$ and the **critical exposure** is $E_c = 10\,\text{mJ/cm}^2$. Calculate the **cure depth** $C_d$. If the layer thickness is set to **half the cure depth**, what is the layer thickness? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**SLS — Principle:**
- SLS is a **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** process that uses a **CO₂ laser** to selectively scan and **sinter** (not fully melt) regions of a polymer powder bed (e.g., PA12).
- The build platform is first coated with a thin layer of powder via a **recoater**.
- The laser scans the cross-section, raising the local temperature above the **glass transition** and approaching the **melting point** of the polymer, causing particles to **fuse** at their contact points.
- After each layer, the platform lowers, fresh powder is spread, and the cycle repeats.

**SLS — Materials:**
- **Polyamide (PA12, PA11)** — most common.
- **TPU (thermoplastic polyurethane)** — for flexible parts.
- **Polystyrene, polypropylene, PEEK** — for special applications.
- **Composite powders** (e.g., glass-filled, carbon-filled nylon).
- **Sand** (for foundry moulds).

**SLS — Advantages:**
1. **No support structures needed** — unfused powder acts as self-support.
2. **Good mechanical properties** (isotropic, near-injection-moulded strength).
3. Multiple parts can be **nested** in the build volume for high throughput.
4. Wide material selection including engineering polymers.

**SLS — Applications:**
- **Functional prototypes** for testing fit and function.
- **Low-volume end-use parts** (small-batch production).
- **Medical devices** (custom orthotics).
- **Aerospace interior components.**

**Comparison SLS vs. SLA:**

| Parameter | SLS | SLA |
|---|---|---|
| State of feedstock | Powder | Liquid resin |
| Energy source | CO₂ laser | UV laser / DLP projector |
| Support | Not required | Required for overhangs |
| Accuracy | ±100 µm | ±25 µm |
| Surface finish | Rougher (grainy) | Smoothest |
| Materials | Engineering thermoplastics | Photopolymers only |
| Anisotropy | Lower (more isotropic) | Higher (layer-dependent) |

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Beer-Lambert cure-depth formula:**
$$
C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$
C_d = 0.18 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{30}{10}\right) = 0.18 \cdot \ln(3)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute logarithm:**
$$
\ln(3) \approx 1.0986
$$

**Step 4 — Final cure depth:**
$$
C_d = 0.18 \cdot 1.0986 \approx 0.1977\,\text{mm} \approx 0.198\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Layer thickness at half the cure depth:**
$$
h = \dfrac{C_d}{2} = \dfrac{0.198}{2} \approx 0.099\,\text{mm} \approx 100\,\mu\text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Choosing the layer thickness as half the cure depth ensures that the **interface** between consecutive layers receives enough exposure to **fully cure** and bond properly.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "SLS" with "SLM/DMLS"** — SLS **sinters** below melting point (PA12 is never melted); SLM/DMLS **fully melts** the powder (metals like Ti6Al4V).
> 2. **Wrong units in VED** — J/mm³ (correct) vs. J/m³ (wrong) vs. J/mm² (wrong — that is AED).
> 3. **Mixing up Vat Photopolymerization and Material Jetting** — both use photopolymers, but VP uses a **vat of liquid** with UV scanning, while MJ uses **droplet jetting + UV flash**.
> 4. **Forgetting conversion from µm to mm** — a single missed conversion causes a 1000× error in VED or cure-depth problems.
> 5. **Listing only "advantages" of a process and not "disadvantages/applications"** — KTU frequently asks for all four (principle, materials, advantages, applications) in a 7-mark part.
> 6. **Writing "SLA uses plastic"** — be specific: **photopolymer resin**.
> 7. **Confusing EBM (electron beam, vacuum, Ti) with SLM (laser, inert gas, any metal)**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3**

- ✅ There are **7 AM process families** in ISO/ASTM 52900: **VP, ME, MJ, BJ, PBF, DED, SL**.
- ✅ **SLA** = UV laser + liquid photopolymer resin (VP family); gives **best surface finish**.
- ✅ **DLP** = projected UV mask (faster than SLA, same family).
- ✅ **FDM/FFF** = thermoplastic filament through heated nozzle (ME family); **lowest cost**, widest material choice.
- ✅ **PolyJet/MJM** = inkjet droplet deposition of photopolymer + UV cure (MJ family); enables **multi-material and full colour** parts.
- ✅ **Binder Jetting** = liquid binder deposited onto powder bed; **no support needed**; requires post-sinter or post-infiltrate for metals.
- ✅ **PBF** includes **SLS (polymer, sintering)** and **SLM/DMLS (metal, full melting)** and **EBM (electron beam, vacuum)**.
- ✅ **DED** = powder or wire fed through nozzle + laser/arc melting; best for **large parts, repair, gradient materials, multi-axis**.
- ✅ **WAAM** is a sub-type of DED using **wire + arc** for large steel/Ti structural parts.
- ✅ **Sheet Lamination (LOM)** = paper/metal foil rolled and laser-cut; cheap and large.
- ✅ **VED** $= \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — the **master process-window metric** for SLM/DMLS/EBM.
- ✅ **AED** $= \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ — used to compare single-layer energy.
- ✅ **LED** $= \dfrac{P}{v}$ — energy per unit length.
- ✅ **Cure depth** in SLA: $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0/E_c)$ — set layer thickness ≈ $C_d/2$.
- ✅ **Staircase error** $= h \cdot \tan\theta$ — drives orientation & surface finish trade-off.
- ✅ **Key process differences** to remember:
    - **SLS vs. SLM:** sinter vs. melt.
    - **PBF vs. DED:** powder bed vs. nozzle feed.
    - **VP vs. MJ:** vat + scanning UV vs. droplet jetting + UV flash.
    - **BJ vs. PBF:** chemical binder vs. thermal fusion.
- ✅ **Self-supporting AM:** SLS, BJ, SL — all use surrounding material as support.
- ✅ **Multi-material capability:** **MJ (PolyJet)** — best in class.
- ✅ **Largest build envelopes:** **BJ (sand)**, **DED (WAAM)**, **LOM**.
- ✅ **Most common in aerospace & medical implants:** **SLM/DMLS/EBM**.
- ✅ **Most common in office prototyping:** **FDM**.
- ✅ **Most common in dental/medical:** **SLA/DLP + DLP bioresin**; **SLM for metal implants**.
- ✅ **Most common in foundry:** **BJ sand moulds**; **LOM paper patterns**.
- ✅ **Choose process based on:** **material → size → accuracy → mechanical property → cost**.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Principle, Materials, Process Parameters, Advantages and Applications of Various AM Processes**
> These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern for **OEMET616 — Additive Manufacturing**. Combine this module with Module 1 (Introduction to AM) and Module 2 (Software & Data Preparation) for a complete pre-exam review.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET616 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** is a powder-bed fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing process defined under **ASTM F2792** as an additive manufacturing process in which thermal energy selectively fuses regions of a powder bed, using a computer-controlled laser beam to sinter successive layers of powdered material (polymer, metal, or ceramic) to form a solid three-dimensional object.

In the **2024 Scheme KTU syllabus** context (Module 3: Powder Bed Fusion Processes), SLS is treated as the *flagship polymer/metal sintering process* that operates on the principle of **partial melting (sintering) below the melting point** for polymers and **full melting followed by solid-state densification** for metals (often called DMLS — Direct Metal Laser Sintering — as a commercial variant).

The standard SLS system consists of:
- A **build chamber** with a heated powder bed
- A **CO₂ laser** (10.6 μm wavelength) for polymers, or a **fiber/Yb-fiber laser** (~1070 nm) for metals
- A **galvanometer-based scanning system**
- A **roller or blade recoater** for layer deposition
- A **build platform** that moves vertically in the Z-axis
- An **inert gas atmosphere** (N₂ for polymers, N₂/Ar for metals)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Snowball Compaction" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine building a sandcastle with a magic magnifying glass**: Instead of pouring wet sand, you have a tray of *dry, loose plastic powder* (like fine flour). A computer-controlled laser — your "magic glass" — moves over the powder, *just heating* each spot enough to make the powder particles *fuse together* (like how two ice cubes briefly touched together become one). Once one thin "frosted" layer is finished, a roller spreads a fresh layer of powder on top, the laser fuses the next cross-section, and the part slowly grows downward into the powder bed. The unfused powder acts as its own support — *you don't need scaffolding at all!*

This is the genius of SLS — the **un-sintered powder surrounding the part is the natural support structure**, allowing complex geometries, lattices, and overhangs to be printed without dedicated support material.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Laser Power (P)** | 5–200 W (polymer: 5–30 W; metal: 100–1000 W) | Energy input per unit time |
| **Scan Speed (v)** | 200–2000 mm/s | Velocity of laser spot |
| **Hatch Spacing (h)** | 0.1–0.5 mm | Distance between adjacent scan vectors |
| **Layer Thickness (t)** | 0.05–0.15 mm | Z-resolution per pass |
| **Build Volume** | Up to **550 × 550 × 750 mm³** (EOS P 770) | Maximum part envelope |
| **Build Chamber Temp.** | **170–180 °C** (PA12) | Just below melting point to reduce thermal stress |
| **Achievable Tolerance** | ± **0.2–0.3 mm** (standard), ± **0.1 mm** with optimization | Dimensional accuracy |
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | **6–12 μm** | As-built surface quality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: The chamber is pre-heated to *just below* the polymer's melting point. This is the single most important reason SLS produces parts with low residual stress and no warping — a frequent 3-mark short-answer question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SLS Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The SLS process follows a strict six-stage cyclic sequence. Understanding the *energy transfer chain* is critical for KTU questions on process parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 — Chamber Initialization**
> The build platform is lowered by exactly **one layer thickness (t)** from the top. The build chamber is filled with **nitrogen (N₂)** gas (for polymers) to prevent oxidation. The chamber temperature is raised to **T_c ≈ T_m − 5 °C** to **T_m − 10 °C** (just below melting point).

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 2 — Powder Deposition**
> A **counter-rotating roller** or **doctor blade** traverses the powder supply chamber, sweeping a precise **layer thickness (t = 80–120 μm typical for PA12)** of fresh powder across the build platform. Excess powder is collected in the overflow bin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 3 — Laser Scanning (Sintering)**
> A **CO₂ laser (λ = 10.6 μm)** is steered by a **galvanometer (galvo) mirror system** across the powder bed. At each X-Y coordinate dictated by the sliced CAD data, the laser delivers energy that **locally heats** the powder above the **sintering onset temperature (T_s)** but **below the melting point (T_m)** for polymers. For metals, the laser fully melts a localized region, and the surrounding powder conducts heat away, causing rapid solidification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 4 — Solidification & Bonding**
> For **polymers**: adjacent particles fuse at the molecular level via *diffusion bonding* and *neck growth* between particles (per Frenkel sintering model). The part gains ~50–60% theoretical density. For **metals**: full melting → solidification creates a dense track.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 5 — Platform Lowering & Recoat**
> The build piston descends by one layer thickness. The roller deposits a fresh layer. The cycle repeats for **N = H/t layers**, where H is the part height.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 6 — Cooling & Part Extraction**
> After the final layer, the build chamber **cools slowly (over 8–24 hours)** to prevent warping and differential shrinkage. Operators then excavate the part from the powder cake, blast off loose powder, and proceed to post-processing.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why preheat the chamber?** To minimize the **thermal gradient (ΔT)** between the sintered layer and the surrounding powder. A smaller ΔT → less shrinkage → less residual stress → less warping. This is the key advantage of SLS over FDM.
- **Why use a CO₂ laser for polymers?** The **10.6 μm wavelength** is strongly absorbed by polymers (high absorption coefficient α ≈ 500–1000 cm⁻¹), unlike metals which require shorter wavelengths.
- **Why no support structures?** The surrounding unsintered powder bed has a **bulk density of ~0.45 g/cm³** for PA12, which provides sufficient mechanical support to resist the weight and internal stresses of the part during build.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are universally tested in KTU University Exam Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on SLS.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** — the most important SLS equation | J/mm³ |
| 2 | $P = E_d \cdot v \cdot h \cdot t$ | Rearranged VED to find required laser power | W |
| 3 | $t_{build} = \dfrac{N \cdot t_{layer}}{3600}$ | Build time estimation (ignoring recoat time) | hours |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{H}{t_{layer}}$ | Number of layers in part of height H | dimensionless |
| 5 | $A_{scan} = v \cdot d$ | Effective scan area per unit time (d = spot diameter) | mm²/s |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{\rho_{actual}}{\rho_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Part density / densification ratio | % |
| 7 | $\Delta T = T_m - T_c$ | Critical thermal gradient (typically 5–10 °C) | °C |
| 8 | $E_{line} = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | **Linear Energy Density (LED)** | J/mm |
| 9 | $E_{area} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | **Areal Energy Density (AED)** | J/mm² |
| 10 | $T_{scan} = \dfrac{L_{part}}{v}$ | Time to scan one vector of length $L_{part}$ | s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Absolute Value Bars in Tables**: When writing $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table, ALWAYS use the LaTeX commands $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of the literal pipe character $\vert$. The pipe character will break the markdown table parser and cause rendering failures in your answer sheet.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: SLS is used to produce **lightweight lattice structures** for aircraft interior brackets (e.g., Airbus A350) where weight reduction is critical.
- **Automotive**: Rapid prototyping of functional plastic components for **form-fit testing** before injection molding.
- **Medical**: Patient-specific **anatomical models** from CT/MRI data for surgical planning; custom orthotics and prosthetics.
- **Foundry**: SLS patterns are used as **investment casting masters** for complex metal parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) from First Principles

The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the cornerstone metric for SLS part quality. It is derived by considering the energy delivered by the laser to a unit volume of powder.

### Step 1 — Energy Delivered per Unit Time
The laser emits energy at a constant rate equal to its power $P$. Thus, the energy delivered per unit time is:

$$E_{rate} = P \quad [\text{Watts} = \text{J/s}]$$

**Logic**: Power is defined as energy per unit time, so the laser's energy emission rate is simply its rated power in Watts.

### Step 2 — Time Required to Scan a Unit Voxel
Consider a single voxel of the powder bed with dimensions:
- Length along scan direction: $\Delta x$ (effectively, the scan line element)
- Width perpendicular to scan: $h$ (hatch spacing)
- Height in Z: $t$ (layer thickness)

The volume of this voxel is:

$$V_{voxel} = h \cdot t \cdot \Delta x$$

**Logic**: We choose a small segment of the scan line of length $\Delta x$. The laser must traverse this segment, and the energy spreads across the hatch spacing $h$ and the layer depth $t$.

### Step 3 — Time to Cross the Voxel
The laser moves with scan velocity $v$, so the time taken to cross the segment of length $\Delta x$ is:

$$\Delta t = \dfrac{\Delta x}{v}$$

**Logic**: Basic kinematic relation — time equals distance divided by speed.

### Step 4 — Energy Delivered to the Voxel
The energy absorbed by the voxel (assuming 100% absorption for simplicity) is:

$$E_{voxel} = P \cdot \Delta t = P \cdot \dfrac{\Delta x}{v}$$

**Logic**: Energy is power multiplied by time of exposure.

### Step 5 — Energy per Unit Volume (VED)
Dividing the energy delivered by the voxel volume:

$$E_d = \dfrac{E_{voxel}}{V_{voxel}} = \dfrac{P \cdot \dfrac{\Delta x}{v}}{h \cdot t \cdot \Delta x}$$

Cancel the $\Delta x$ terms in numerator and denominator:

$$\boxed{E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

**Logic**: After cancellation, the segment length $\Delta x$ drops out, leaving a pure volumetric quantity — the energy density. The sign convention and units (J/mm³) follow naturally.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem**: An SLS machine uses a **CO₂ laser of power P = 30 W** to sinter **PA12 powder**. The scan speed is **v = 5000 mm/s**, the hatch spacing is **h = 0.25 mm**, and the layer thickness is **t = 0.10 mm**. Calculate the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** delivered to the powder bed.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Identify the given parameters.

$$P = 30 \text{ W}, \quad v = 5000 \text{ mm/s}, \quad h = 0.25 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 0.10 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2: Substitute into the VED equation.

$$E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

Step 3: Compute the denominator.

$$v \cdot h \cdot t = 5000 \times 0.25 \times 0.10$$

$$\begin{aligned}
v \cdot h \cdot t &= 5000 \times 0.025 \\
&= 125 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4: Compute the energy density.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_d &= \dfrac{30}{125} \\
&= 0.24 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

Step 5: Interpretation.

> **Result**: The VED is **0.24 J/mm³**. For PA12, the optimal sintering window is **E_d ≈ 0.20 to 0.35 J/mm³**. Since 0.24 J/mm³ lies within this window, the parameters are well-chosen for densification without polymer degradation.

## 3.3 Build Time Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem**: A part has a total height of **H = 80 mm**. The layer thickness is **t = 0.10 mm**. Calculate the number of layers and the *pure scan time* if each layer requires **t_layer = 25 s** of laser operation (recoat time neglected).

**Solution:**

Step 1: Number of layers.

$$N = \dfrac{H}{t} = \dfrac{80}{0.10} = 800 \text{ layers}$$

Step 2: Total pure scan time.

$$T_{scan} = N \cdot t_{layer} = 800 \times 25 = 20000 \text{ s}$$

Step 3: Convert to hours.

$$T_{scan} = \dfrac{20000}{3600} \approx 5.56 \text{ hours}$$

**Note**: The actual machine cycle time will be 30–50% longer due to **recoating, idle time between layers, and post-build cooling (8–24 hours)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SLS vs. SLA vs. FDM

| Parameter | SLS | SLA (Stereolithography) | FDM (Fused Deposition) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | CO₂ / Fiber Laser | UV Laser | Heated Nozzle |
| State of Material | Powder (solid) | Liquid Photopolymer | Solid Filament |
| Support Required? | **No** (powder bed supports) | Yes (scaffolds) | Yes |
| Material Wastage | Powder **98% recyclable** | Resin cures — partially wasted | Filament minimal |
| Surface Roughness | **6–12 μm** | **1–5 μm** | **20–200 μm** |
| Anisotropy | Low–Moderate | Low | **High** |
| Typical Tolerance | ± 0.2 mm | ± 0.1 mm | ± 0.5 mm |
| Material Options | Polymer, Metal, Ceramic, Sand, Glass | Photopolymers only | Thermoplastics only |
| Build Speed | Moderate | Slow–Moderate | Fast |
| Cost per Part | High (powder + gas) | Moderate | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow Diagram — SLS Layer-by-Layer Operation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the cyclic workflow of the SLS process, from CAD input to finished part.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: CAD Model Input] --> B[StepB: STL Slicing into Layers]
    B --> C[StepC: Initialize Build Chamber with N2 Gas]
    C --> D[StepD: Preheat Powder Bed to Tc just below Tm]
    D --> E[StepE: Lower Build Platform by Layer Thickness t]
    E --> F[StepF: Roller Deposits Fresh Powder Layer]
    F --> G[StepG: CO2 Laser Scans Cross-Section per Slice Data]
    G --> H[StepH: Local Sintering of Powder Particles]
    H --> I[StepI: Solidification and Inter-Particle Neck Growth]
    I --> J{StepJ: All Layers Complete?}
    J -->|No| E
    J -->|Yes| K[StepK: Slow Controlled Cooling of Chamber]
    K --> L[StepL: Part Excavation from Powder Cake]
    L --> M[StepM: Powder Removal via Bead Blasting]
    M --> N[StepN: Optional Post-Processing: Infiltration / Dyeing / Machining]
    N --> O[StepO: Finished Functional Part]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — SLS System Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the **functional subsystems** of a commercial SLS machine (e.g., EOS P 770, 3D Systems sPro 230).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerSupply
        PS1[PS1: CO2 Laser 10.6 micrometer 30 to 200 W]
    end
    subgraph OpticalTrain
        OT1[OT1: Beam Expander and Collimator]
        OT2[OT2: Galvanometer X-Y Mirror Scanner]
        OT3[OT3: F-Theta Focusing Lens]
    end
    subgraph PowderDelivery
        PD1[PD1: Powder Supply Chamber]
        PD2[PD2: Counter Rotating Roller Recoater]
        PD3[PD3: Overflow Collection Bin]
    end
    subgraph BuildVolume
        BV1[BV1: Heated Build Platform with Z-Actuator]
        BV2[BV2: Build Chamber with N2 Inert Atmosphere]
    end
    subgraph ThermalControl
        TC1[TC1: Chamber Heaters 170 to 180 degree Celsius]
        TC2[TC2: IR Temperature Sensors and PID Controller]
    end
    subgraph ControlSystem
        CS1[CS1: Industrial PC with Slicing Software]
        CS2[CS2: Real Time Process Monitoring and Closed Loop Feedback]
    end
    PS1 --> OT1
    OT1 --> OT2
    OT2 --> OT3
    OT3 --> BV1
    PD1 --> PD2
    PD2 --> BV1
    BV1 --> PD3
    TC1 --> BV2
    TC2 --> CS2
    CS1 --> OT2
    CS1 --> PD2
    CS1 --> BV1
    CS2 --> PS1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Energy Transfer Block Diagram

This diagram traces the **thermodynamic energy chain** from electrical input to densified part.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[E1: Electrical Input 220 V AC Mains] --> E2[E2: Laser Diode / CO2 Tube converts to Coherent Light]
    E2 --> E3[E3: Optical Train delivers Beam to Powder Bed]
    E3 --> E4[E4: Powder Surface Absorbs Wavelength 10.6 micrometer for Polymer]
    E4 --> E5[E5: Photon Energy converted to Heat above Ts Sintering Onset]
    E5 --> E6[E6: Neck Growth and Diffusion Bonding between Particles]
    E6 --> E7[E7: Partial Densification 50 to 60 percent for PA12]
    E7 --> E8[E8: Optional Infiltration with Epoxy or Bronze to reach 95 percent Density]
    E8 --> E9[E9: Functional Solid Part Ready for Service]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: Drawing the **energy transfer chain** (electrical → optical → thermal → mechanical) is a high-scoring strategy for the 7-mark "Explain the SLS process" question. It demonstrates engineering-level understanding, not just rote memorization.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Selective Laser Sintering. Mention any two commonly used materials. [3 Marks]**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**
Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) is a powder-bed fusion additive manufacturing process (as per ASTM F2792) in which a computer-controlled laser selectively fuses regions of a powder bed to form a solid 3D object layer by layer. The process operates just below the melting point of the material, causing particles to bond via diffusion and neck growth without complete melting.

**Two commonly used materials**:
1. **Polyamide 12 (PA12 / Nylon 12)** — the industry standard polymer for SLS.
2. **Stainless Steel 316L (SS316L)** powder — for metal SLS / DMLS variants.

> [!NOTE]
> Other acceptable answers: **PA11, PA2200, Glass-filled Nylon, TPU elastomers, AlSi10Mg, Ti6Al4V, Inconel 718, Sand with binder, Polystyrene (PS) for casting patterns**.

---

### **Question 2: What is the role of the inert nitrogen atmosphere in the SLS process? [3 Marks]**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**
The build chamber is flooded with **nitrogen (N₂) gas** to displace oxygen and prevent **oxidative degradation** of the polymer powder during laser sintering. At the elevated chamber temperature (170–180 °C for PA12), the presence of oxygen would cause **thermal-oxidative chain scission**, leading to:
1. Discoloration (yellowing/browning) of the part.
2. Embrittlement and loss of mechanical strength (reduced tensile strength and elongation at break).
3. Generation of hazardous fumes.

For **metal SLS**, nitrogen (or argon) prevents **oxidation of the metal melt pool**, which would otherwise form oxide inclusions that compromise fatigue life and fracture toughness.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule**: Every Part B question in the End Semester Examination offers the student an *internal choice*. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below for parallel practice.

---

### **Question A: 14 Marks**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Module 3]`**

#### **(a) Explain the Selective Laser Sintering process in detail, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of process parameters on part quality. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) is a powder-bed fusion additive manufacturing process that uses a laser to selectively sinter successive layers of polymer or metal powder to build a 3D part, as defined in ASTM F2792.

**Process Steps (4 marks):**
1. **CAD to STL conversion** and slicing into 2D cross-sections.
2. **Build chamber preheating** to T_c ≈ T_m − 5 to 10 °C, filled with N₂.
3. **Powder deposition** by a counter-rotating roller to a layer thickness t = 80–120 μm.
4. **Laser scanning** using a CO₂ laser (10.6 μm) steered by galvanometer mirrors, delivering energy density E_d = P/(v·h·t).
5. **Sintering** of polymer particles (neck growth, diffusion bonding) at T_s < T_m.
6. **Platform lowering** by t; recoat with fresh powder; cycle repeats for N = H/t layers.
7. **Slow cooling** (8–24 hours) to prevent warping, followed by powder removal.

**Role of Process Parameters on Part Quality (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect of Increase |
|---|---|
| Laser Power (P) | ↑ density up to a limit, then **degradation** / curling |
| Scan Speed (v) | ↓ energy input, ↑ productivity, may cause **porosity** |
| Hatch Spacing (h) | ↑ → **un-sintered gaps** between vectors, ↓ density |
| Layer Thickness (t) | ↑ → faster build but **stair-step effect**, ↓ resolution |
| Bed Temp (T_c) | ↑ closer to T_m → ↑ bonding but risk of **"growth"** phenomenon |

**Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4).**

---

#### **(b) Derive the expression for Volumetric Energy Density (VED) in SLS. A machine operates at P = 50 W, v = 4000 mm/s, h = 0.20 mm, and t = 0.10 mm. Compute the VED and comment on its adequacy for PA12. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final result is:

$$E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Numerical Solution (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_d &= \dfrac{50}{4000 \times 0.20 \times 0.10} \\
&= \dfrac{50}{80} \\
&= 0.625 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on Adequacy (1 mark):**
The optimal VED window for PA12 is **0.20 to 0.35 J/mm³**. The computed value of **0.625 J/mm³ is significantly higher than the upper limit**, indicating:
- **Excessive energy input** → risk of **polymer degradation, yellowing, and dimensional inaccuracy** due to over-sintering.
- **Recommendation**: Reduce laser power to ~20 W OR increase scan speed to ~12500 mm/s to bring VED into the optimal window.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3]`**

#### **(a) Compare Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) with Stereolithography (SLA) and Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM). Discuss the advantages and limitations of SLS. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparative Table (3 marks):** Refer to the **Analytical Comparison Matrix** in **Section 3.4** of these notes.

**Advantages of SLS (2.5 marks):**
1. **No support structures required** — surrounding powder bed acts as natural support, enabling complex lattices, overhangs, and internal channels.
2. **Wide material range** — polymers, metals, ceramics, glass, sand, composites.
3. **High powder recyclability** — up to 98% of unfused powder is reused in the next build, reducing material waste.
4. **Good mechanical properties** — near-isotropic parts (low anisotropy), suitable for functional prototypes and end-use parts.
5. **Minimal warping** — chamber preheating eliminates the thermal gradient that causes warping in FDM.

**Limitations of SLS (1.5 marks):**
1. **High equipment and material cost** — SLS systems cost ₹50 lakh to ₹5 crore; PA12 powder costs ₹8,000–15,000 per kg.
2. **Porous parts** — as-built SLS parts have **40–50% porosity**, requiring post-processing (infiltration) for fully dense parts.
3. **Rough surface finish** — Ra = 6–12 μm, not suitable for optical or aesthetic surfaces without post-processing.
4. **Long cooling cycle** — 8–24 hours of post-build cooling increases total lead time.
5. **Limited color options** — parts are typically white/gray/off-white from the natural powder.

---

#### **(b) A polymer SLS part of height H = 60 mm is built with layer thickness t = 0.12 mm and hatch spacing h = 0.30 mm. The laser power is P = 25 W and scan speed v = 6000 mm/s. Determine: (i) the number of layers, (ii) the VED, and (iii) the linear energy density. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**

$$H = 60 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 0.12 \text{ mm}, \quad h = 0.30 \text{ mm}, \quad P = 25 \text{ W}, \quad v = 6000 \text{ mm/s}$$

**(i) Number of Layers (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{H}{t} \\
&= \dfrac{60}{0.12} \\
&= 500 \text{ layers}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Volumetric Energy Density (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_d &= \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\
&= \dfrac{25}{6000 \times 0.30 \times 0.12} \\
&= \dfrac{25}{216} \\
&\approx 0.1157 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Linear Energy Density (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{line} &= \dfrac{P}{v} \\
&= \dfrac{25}{6000} \\
&\approx 4.167 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:**
The VED of **0.116 J/mm³ is below the optimal window** of 0.20–0.35 J/mm³ for PA12, suggesting the part will be **under-sintered with high porosity and low strength**. The student should recommend increasing P or decreasing v.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for SLS Questions**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Always convert scan speed to **mm/s** (not m/s) and hatch spacing to **mm** (not μm) before computing VED. Mixing units is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> 2. **Confusing Energy Densities**: Students frequently write **VED = P/(v·h·t)** but mistakenly use it where the question asks for **LED = P/v** or **AED = P/(v·h)**. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting the Density Justification**: A numerical VED problem MUST end with a *comment on adequacy* (1 mark reserved). Writing only the calculation loses this mark.
> 4. **Wrong VED Range for PA12**: The safe window is **0.20 to 0.35 J/mm³** for PA12 nylon. Memorize this.
> 5. **Ignoring the CO₂ Wavelength Justification**: If asked "why CO₂ laser for polymer SLS?", state that the **10.6 μm wavelength is strongly absorbed by polymers (high absorption coefficient)** — not just "it's a laser".
> 6. **Skipping the Block Diagram**: A 7-mark "Explain the SLS process" question *always* allocates **1–2 marks for the diagram**. Drawing the energy chain or system block diagram is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These 12 Bullets**

- ✅ **ASTM F2792** classifies SLS as a **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** process.
- ✅ The **CO₂ laser wavelength (10.6 μm)** is the industry standard for **polymer** SLS.
- ✅ The **chamber is preheated to T_c = T_m − 5 to 10 °C** to prevent warping — this is SLS's key advantage.
- ✅ The **master equation** is $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — *the most tested formula in this module*.
- ✅ **Optimal VED window for PA12 = 0.20 to 0.35 J/mm³**.
- ✅ **No support structures** are needed in SLS — the unfused powder bed is the natural support.
- ✅ **Build chamber atmosphere is N₂** to prevent oxidation; for metals, sometimes Ar is used.
- ✅ **PA12 (Polyamide 12 / Nylon 12)** is the most widely used SLS polymer globally.
- ✅ **DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering)** is the commercial metal-SLS variant using a fiber laser (~1070 nm).
- ✅ As-built SLS polymer parts have **40–50% porosity**; **infiltration with epoxy or bronze** can boost density to ~95%.
- ✅ **Recoat mechanism** is a counter-rotating roller or doctor blade depositing a layer of thickness t.
- ✅ **Surface roughness Ra = 6–12 μm** (SLS) vs. 1–5 μm (SLA) vs. 20–200 μm (FDM) — remember the order.
- ✅ **Key manufacturer names to remember for KTU viva**: EOS GmbH (Germany), 3D Systems (USA), Sintratec (Switzerland), Formlabs Fuse (USA) — for polymer SLS. SLM Solutions, Concept Laser, Renishaw — for metal SLS.
- ✅ The **directional energy chain** to remember: **Electrical → Laser Light → Optical Train → Powder Absorption → Heat → Sintering → Solid Part**.

---
