# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three applications of Additive Manufacturing in the automotive industry. Mention the AM process used in each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Rapid prototyping of dashboard assemblies** — using **SLA (Stereolithography)** for optical clarity and fine detail.
2. **Topology-optimized control arms and subframes** — using **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)** with Ti6Al4V or AlSi10Mg.
3. **Conformal cooling inserts for injection molds** — using **DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering)** to embed curved internal cooling channels.
*(Alternative valid answers accepted: FDM for jigs/fixtures, SLS for functional nylon prototypes, Binder Jetting for large aluminum brackets.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Conformal Cooling" and explain its advantages over conventional straight-drilled cooling in automotive injection molds.**

**Model Answer:**
**Conformal Cooling** is a Design-for-AM technique where cooling channels follow the **3D contour of the molded part cavity**, made possible only because AM can print internal curved passages impossible to drill by conventional methods.

**Advantages:**
- **Uniform temperature distribution** → reduced warpage.
- **Shorter cycle time (20–40 % reduction)** → higher production rate.
- **Improved part quality** → fewer weld lines and sink marks.
- **Longer mold life** → reduced thermal fatigue.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — **Option 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the role of Additive Manufacturing in the automotive product development cycle. Discuss its application in **(i) Concept prototyping, (ii) Tooling, and (iii) End-use part production** with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel brake caliper originally weighing **4.5 kg** is redesigned using topology optimization and printed in **AlSi10Mg** ($\rho = 2680\;\text{kg/m}^3$) with lattice infill reducing the effective volume to **40 %** of the bounding box. If the bounding-box volume is $V_{\text{bb}} = 800\;\text{cm}^3$, calculate:
- (i) Mass of the AM brake caliper.
- (ii) Percentage mass reduction over the original steel part ($\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7850\;\text{kg/m}^3$).
- (iii) Build time if $R_{\text{dep}} = 6\;\text{cm}^3/\text{hr}$.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A**

#### **Part (a) — Model Answer [7 Marks]:**

The automotive product development cycle consists of **Concept → Design → Prototype → Tooling → Production → Certification**. AM contributes at every stage:

**(i) Concept Prototyping [2 Marks]:**
- Visualization models for design reviews using **SLA** (clear resin) or **FDM** (low-cost).
- Allows styling engineers to evaluate **A-class surfaces** within days.
- Example: Exterior mirror housings, dashboard knobs, and steering column covers.

**(ii) Tooling [2 Marks]:**
- **3D-printed sand molds** for prototyping cylinder heads and intake manifolds.
- **Conformal cooling inserts** in steel injection molds using DMLS, reducing cycle time by 20–40 %.
- **Soft tooling** for short-run sheet-metal stamping dies (3–500 parts).
- Example: Bugatti uses DMLS-printed Ti conformal-cooled brake caliper mold inserts.

**(iii) End-Use Part Production [3 Marks]:**
- **Bugatti Chiron** — world's first 3D-printed **functionally optimized titanium brake caliper**, 41 % lighter, 20 % stronger than aluminum.
- **Czinger 21C** — most of the chassis is AM-printed; weighs under **1250 kg** despite 1250 hp.
- **Lightning Motorcycles** — 3D-printed battery housings for EVs.
- **Divergent 3D / Blade** — entire chassis printed in metal nodes joined with carbon tubes.

**Concluding Statement:** AM enables a *closed-loop mass-customization* paradigm where each vehicle can have bespoke parts without re-tooling.

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- Original mass: $m_{\text{orig}} = 4.5\;\text{kg}$
- Material: AlSi10Mg, $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2680\;\text{kg/m}^3$
- Steel density: $\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7850\;\text{kg/m}^3$
- Bounding-box volume: $V_{\text{bb}} = 800\;\text{cm}^3$
- Lattice volume fraction: $\bar{\rho}_{\text{lat}} = 0.40$
- Deposition rate: $R_{\text{dep}} = 6\;\text{cm}^3/\text{hr}$

**(i) Mass of AM part [3 Marks]:**

Effective volume:

$$V_{\text{part}} = V_{\text{bb}} \times \bar{\rho}_{\text{lat}}$$

$$V_{\text{part}} = 800 \times 0.40 = 320\;\text{cm}^3$$

Convert to m³:

$$V_{\text{part}} = 320 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^3$$

Mass:

$$m_{\text{AM}} = \rho_{\text{Al}} \times V_{\text{part}}$$

$$m_{\text{AM}} = 2680 \times 320 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$m_{\text{AM}} = 0.8576\;\text{kg}$$

**(ii) Percentage mass reduction [2 Marks]:**

$$\eta = \frac{m_{\text{orig}} - m_{\text{AM}}}{m_{\text{orig}}} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{4.5 - 0.8576}{4.5} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{3.6424}{4.5} \times 100$$

$$\eta \approx 80.94 \;\%$$

**(iii) Build time [2 Marks]:**

$$t_b = \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{R_{\text{dep}}}$$

$$t_b = \frac{320}{6} \approx 53.33\;\text{hours}$$

$$\approx 2.22 \;\text{days}$$

---

### QUESTION B — **Option 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **DfAM workflow for an automotive component**. Explain the role of **Topology Optimization** and **Lattice Structures** in achieving lightweight design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **SLM, SLS, and FDM** for automotive applications based on **(i) materials, (ii) surface finish, (iii) dimensional accuracy, (iv) typical application, and (v) cost per part**. Identify which process is most suitable for printing a **metal engine bracket** for a Formula Student race car.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B**

#### **Part (a) — Model Answer [7 Marks]:**

**DfAM Workflow Block Diagram [3 Marks]:**

```
CAD Model → Design Space Definition → Load & BC Application
→ Material Selection → FEA Solver → Topology Optimization
→ Lattice Generation → DfAM Validation → STL/Slicer Output
→ AM Build → Post-Processing → Final Part
```

**Topology Optimization [2 Marks]:**
- Mathematical technique (SIMP — Solid Isotropic Material with Penalization) that distributes material within a design space such that **stiffness is maximized for a given mass**, or **mass is minimized for a given stiffness**.
- Produces organic, bone-like geometries impossible to manufacture by casting or machining.
- Example: BMW i8 rear roof console bracket — **44 % lighter** with topology optimization.

**Lattice Structures [2 Marks]:**
- Repeating unit cells (BCC, FCC, Gyroid, Octet-Truss) providing high **specific stiffness**.
- Allow **tunable energy absorption** in crash zones.
- **Gyroid** lattice — used in Formula 1 nose cones; absorbs impact while allowing airflow.

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Comparison Table [5 Marks]:**

| Parameter | SLM | SLS | FDM |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| (i) Materials | AlSi10Mg, Ti6Al4V, IN718, 316L | PA12, PA11, TPU, PP | ABS, PLA, PETG, Nylon, PC |
| (ii) Surface Finish (Ra µm) | 5–15 | 5–10 | 8–20 |
| (iii) Dimensional Accuracy | ±0.05 mm | ±0.10 mm | ±0.20 mm |
| (iv) Typical Application | Engine brackets, nodes, calipers | Functional prototypes, ducting | Jigs, fixtures, dashboard mock-ups |
| (v) Cost per Part | Very High (₹₹₹₹) | High (₹₹₹) | Low (₹) |

**Most Suitable Process for Formula Student Metal Engine Bracket [2 Marks]:**

**SLM (Selective Laser Melting)** is the best choice because:
1. **Metal printing** — both SLS and FDM cannot directly print high-strength metals.
2. **Tight tolerance** (±0.05 mm) required for bolted engine interfaces.
3. **High specific strength** achievable with AlSi10Mg or Ti6Al4V.
4. **Topology optimization compatible** — enables the mass reduction critical in Formula Student competition scoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse SLM with SLS** — SLM fully melts metal powder; SLS sinters polymer.
> - **Do NOT state "AM parts need no post-processing"** — every KTU answer must mention support removal, HIP, or stress-relief.
> - **Always include material-specific heat treatment** (e.g., T6 for AlSi10Mg) — marks deducted otherwise.
> - **Numerical answers without units lose marks** — write `kg`, `cm³`, `hours`, `%` explicitly.
> - **Avoid claims like "AM replaces all traditional manufacturing"** — examiners penalize over-generalization. State the **hybrid workflow** instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Automotive AM scope:** Prototyping → Tooling → End-use parts (three-tier lifecycle).
- **Signature benefits:** Mass reduction (30–60 %), part consolidation (8→1), design freedom, mass customization.
- **Flagship automotive AM parts:** Bugatti Ti brake caliper, Czinger 21C chassis, Divergent Blade nodes.
- **Most used processes:** SLM/DMLS (metals), SLS/MJF (polymers), FDM (jigs/fixtures), SLA (visual prototypes).
- **Key formula to memorize:** $m_{\text{AM}} = \rho \cdot V_{\text{bb}} \cdot \bar{\rho}_{\text{lat}}$, $\eta = (m_{\text{orig}} - m_{\text{AM}})/m_{\text{orig}} \times 100$.
- **Build time formula:** $t_b = V_{\text{part}} / R_{\text{dep}}$ (verify whether $h$ is included in $R_{\text{dep}}$ or not).
- **DfAM rules:** Min wall 0.3–0.8 mm (process dependent), 45° overhang rule, escape holes for powder removal.
- **Conformal cooling:** AM-exclusive advantage; cycle time reduction 20–40 %.
- **Certification standards to cite:** ISO/ASTM 52900, IATF 16949, AMS 7000-series for aerospace-grade AM.
- **Sustainability angle (bonus marks):** AM reduces material waste from 70 % (subtractive) to <10 %.
- **KTU buzzwords for full marks:** Topology Optimization, Lattice, Gyroid, Conformal Cooling, DfAM, HIP, IATF 16949, Bionic Design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical and Other Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET616 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Medical and Other Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additive Manufacturing (AM) for Medical & Other Applications** refers to the layer-by-layer fabrication of patient-specific anatomical models, biocompatible implants, surgical guides, functional tissue scaffolds, and complex industrial components directly from digital 3D data (typically derived from CT, MRI, or CAD sources) using processes such as **Stereolithography (SLA)**, **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)**, **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)**, **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)**, **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)**, and **Bioprinting**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this module emphasizes the **functional translation of AM processes** from prototyping to end-use parts across **biomedical, aerospace, automotive, dental, and consumer-product sectors**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Custom Tailor for Everything":**
> Imagine a tailor who can stitch a shirt, a dress, a spacesuit, a surgeon’s glove, and even a piece of artificial bone — all cut exactly to your body measurements, with internal honeycomb structures that no sewing machine could ever replicate. AM is that universal tailor. For medicine, it is the tailor who reads your CT scan and prints a **spinal cage shaped precisely to your vertebrae**. For aerospace, it is the tailor who designs a **fuel nozzle with internal cooling channels** that a traditional casting could never produce. The "fabric" is no longer cloth — it is titanium powder, biocompatible polymer, or even living cells (bio-ink).

### 1.3 Why This Module Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Engineering Metrics Highlighted in KTU Syllabus:**
> - **Biocompatibility (ISO 10993)** — material must not provoke immune response
> - **Pore size for bone scaffolds:** **200–500 µm** (optimal for osteointegration)
> - **Porosity:** typically **40–80 %** for bone ingrowth
> - **Dimensional accuracy:** **±0.05 mm to ±0.1 mm** for medical implants
> - **Surface roughness (Ra):** **5–20 µm** post-processing
> - **Young's modulus matching:** ideally **10–30 GPa** to match cortical bone and avoid stress shielding

### 1.4 Broad Classification of Applications

| Sector | Core Application | Dominant AM Process |
|---|---|---|
| Medical | Patient-specific implants, surgical guides | DMLS, SLA, FDM, Bioprinting |
| Dental | Crowns, bridges, aligners, surgical stents | SLA, DLP, SLS |
| Aerospace | Lightweight lattice brackets, fuel nozzles | SLM, EBM, DMLS |
| Automotive | Functional prototypes, jigs, end-use parts | SLS, MJF, FDM |
| Architecture | Scale models, complex facades | Binder Jetting, FDM |
| Consumer | Jewelry, eyewear, footwear | SLA, DLP, PolyJet |
| Food | 3D printed chocolates, customized nutrition | Food-grade extrusion |
| Defense | Spare parts on demand, ruggedized components | DMLS, SLM |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Medical AM — Patient-Specific Customization

Traditional manufacturing of implants (e.g., hip stems) relied on **stock sizes** — small, medium, large. The patient's bone anatomy rarely matched the implant perfectly, causing:
- Loosening over time
- Stress shielding (stiff implant carries load, surrounding bone resorbs)
- Revision surgeries

AM solves this through:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Customization Chain in Medical AM:**
> 1. **Imaging:** CT / MRI scan of patient anatomy (DICOM data)
> 2. **Segmentation:** Software (Mimics, 3D Slicer) converts scan to STL
> 3. **CAD Modelling:** Implant designed over the anatomical model
> 4. **Lattice Structuring:** Internal porous architecture generated (e.g., gyroid, TPMS)
> 5. **Slicing & Printing:** Build file (.G-code) sent to AM machine
> 6. **Post-processing:** Support removal, HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing), sterilization

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Aerospace & Other Industrial AM

| Engineering Need | AM Solution |
|---|---|
| Weight reduction | Topology-optimized lattice structures (up to **70 % weight savings**) |
| Part consolidation | Multiple parts → single printed part (e.g., GE LEAP fuel nozzle: 18 → 1) |
| Internal cooling channels | Conformal cooling in injection mold inserts (40 % cycle time reduction) |
| On-demand spare parts | Digital inventory for legacy aircraft (Boeing, Airbus) |
| Complex assemblies | Hinges, bearings printed in one shot |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` in tables below instead of the pipe symbol to prevent markdown breakage.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$E^{*} = C \cdot E_{s} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}\right)^{n}$$ | **Gibson-Ashby Model** for cellular/lattice solids. E* = effective modulus, Es = solid modulus, ρ* = lattice density, ρs = solid density. Typically C ≈ 1, n ≈ 2 for open-cell foams | E in **GPa**, ρ in **g/cm³** |
| 2 | $$\sigma^{*}_{pl} = C_{2} \cdot \sigma_{ys} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}\right)^{3/2}$$ | Plastic collapse strength of cellular solid | σ in **MPa** |
| 3 | $$P = 1 - \dfrac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}$$ | **Porosity (P)** of lattice — fraction of void volume | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 4 | $$\dfrac{\sigma^{*}}{\sigma_{s}} = C \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}\right)^{m}$$ | General strength-scaling law for porous structures | m typically 1.5 to 2.0 |
| 5 | $$R_{a} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$$ | **Arithmetic mean surface roughness** — critical for biocompatibility | Ra in **µm** |
| 6 | $$V_{b} = A_{layer} \cdot t_{layer} \cdot f_{corr}$$ | Build volume throughput — A = layer area, t = layer thickness, fcorr = correction factor for support | V in **cm³/h** |
| 7 | $$k = k_{s} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}\right)^{1.65}$$ | Thermal conductivity scaling for porous AM parts | k in **W/m·K** |
| 8 | $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_{nozzle} \cdot v_{dep}$$ | Mass deposition rate for FDM/bioprinting — critical for tissue scaffold build time | ṁ in **g/s** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where These Equations Are Applied in Industry:**
> - **Gibson-Ashby (Eq. 1 & 2):** Designing spinal cages and acetabular cups with modulus matched to cortical bone (**~15 GPa**) to avoid stress shielding.
> - **Porosity (Eq. 3):** Quality control of titanium acetabular cups (e.g., Lima Corporate, Stryker) — porosity typically **60–80 %**.
> - **Surface roughness (Eq. 5):** Post-processing of dental crowns — SLA delivers **Ra ≈ 5 µm**, but abutment surfaces require **Ra < 1 µm** for plaque resistance.
> - **Build volume (Eq. 6):** Cost estimation in aerospace — GE Aviation uses DMLS for fuel nozzles with build rates of **~10 cm³/h**.

### 2.5 The Stress Shielding Problem (Why Modulus Matching Matters)

> [!WARNING]
> **Stress Shielding:** A stiff metal implant (Ti-6Al-4V solid: **E ≈ 110 GPa**) carries most of the physiological load, leaving surrounding bone under-stressed. According to **Wolff's Law**, bone resorbs when unloaded, leading to implant loosening. **Lattice AM structures** reduce effective modulus to **~10–30 GPa**, matching cortical bone and preserving bone density.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Modulus of a Lattice Scaffold (Gibson-Ashby)

The **Gibson-Ashby model** is the KTU-favoured analytical tool for predicting mechanical behaviour of AM lattice structures. We will derive the relationship from beam-bending mechanics.

**Step 1 — Consider a single strut of a cellular solid.**

Let the strut have length $L_{s}$, square cross-section $t \times t$, and be loaded as a beam under axial and bending actions. The second moment of area for a square cross-section is:

$$
I = \frac{t^{4}}{12}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the strain energy in a single bent strut.**

For a beam bent by displacement $\delta$ at its tip (cantilever assumption), strain energy is:

$$
U = \frac{3 \cdot E_{s} \cdot I \cdot \delta^{2}}{2 \cdot L_{s}^{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate macroscopic stress to micro-structural geometry.**

The relative density of an open-cell foam (cubic cell model) is:

$$
\frac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}} \propto \left(\frac{t}{L_{s}}\right)^{2}
$$

So the strut slenderness ratio is:

$$
\frac{t}{L_{s}} = \left(\frac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}\right)^{1/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the bending stiffness of a single cell.**

The effective bending stiffness of a unit cell, $K_{cell}$, scales with the strut's $E_s I$ divided by cell size:

$$
K_{cell} = C_{1} \cdot \frac{E_{s} \cdot t^{4}}{L_{s}^{3}}
$$

Substituting $t = L_s (\rho^*/\rho_s)^{1/2}$:

$$
K_{cell} = C_{1} \cdot \frac{E_{s} \cdot L_{s}^{4} \cdot (\rho^{*}/\rho_{s})^{2}}{L_{s}^{3}} = C_{1} \cdot E_{s} \cdot L_{s} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum over unit cells per unit area to get macroscopic modulus.**

Macroscopic stress $\sigma^{*}$ relates to macroscopic strain $\varepsilon^{*}$ through the cell stiffness per unit area. For a regular cubic cell arrangement, the number of cells per unit area is $1/L_s^2$, giving:

$$
E^{*} = C \cdot E_{s} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}\right)^{2}
$$

This is the classic **Gibson-Ashby square-law**, rigorously derived. For open-cell foams $n \approx 2$; for closed-cell foams $n \approx 2$ to $3$.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

> Given a Ti-6Al-4V spinal cage with relative density $\rho^*/\rho_s = 0.30$, $E_s = 110$ GPa, and constant $C = 1$:
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> E^{*} &= 1 \cdot 110 \cdot (0.30)^{2} \\
> E^{*} &= 110 \cdot 0.09 \\
> E^{*} &= 9.9 \text{ GPa}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
> 
> **Result:** The lattice cage has effective modulus **9.9 GPa**, closely matching cortical bone (**10–30 GPa**). This eliminates stress shielding — a critical KTU evaluation point.

### 3.2 Derivation: Plastic Collapse Strength Scaling

For yielding (instead of linear elastic behaviour), the bending moment per strut must exceed the plastic moment $M_p = \sigma_{ys} \cdot t^3/4$. Following the same dimensional argument:

$$
\sigma_{pl}^{*} = C_{2} \cdot \sigma_{ys} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho^{*}}{\rho_{s}}\right)^{3/2}
$$

**Worked Example:** For the same spinal cage with $\sigma_{ys} = 880$ MPa, $C_2 = 0.3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{pl}^{*} &= 0.3 \cdot 880 \cdot (0.30)^{3/2} \\
&= 264 \cdot 0.1643 \\
&\approx 43.4 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

This satisfies the **ASTM F2077 fatigue requirement of 40 MPa** for intervertebral body fusion devices — a typical KTU board question benchmark.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: AM Process Selection for Medical Applications

| AM Process | Medical Use Case | Material | Resolution (XY) | Biocompatibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SLA** | Dental crowns, surgical guides, hearing-aid shells | Photopolymer resin (MED610) | **25–50 µm** | Yes (USP Class VI) |
| **DLP** | Dental aligners, anatomical models | Photopolymer | **35–100 µm** | Yes |
| **SLS** | Orthopedic jigs, custom prosthetics | Nylon-12 (PA2200), TPU | **100–150 µm** | Yes (PA2200 certified) |
| **FDM** | Surgical cutting guides, anatomical models | PLA, ABS, PEEK | **200–400 µm** | PEEK biocompatible |
| **DMLS / SLM** | Titanium spinal cages, acetabular cups, dental implants | Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 23), CoCr | **30–80 µm** | **Excellent** |
| **EBM** | Large orthopedic implants, acetabular shells | Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5) | **100–200 µm** | Excellent |
| **PolyJet** | Multi-material anatomical models, soft-tissue phantoms | Acrylic photopolymers | **14–28 µm** | Limited |
| **Bioprinting (Extrusion)** | Skin, cartilage, bone scaffolds | Hydrogels, bio-inks (alginate, GelMA) | **100–500 µm** | Living-cell compatible |
| **Bioprinting (DLP)** | Vascular networks, organoids | PEGDA, GelMA | **10–50 µm** | Living-cell compatible |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Aerospace & Automotive AM Applications

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. What is bioprinting, and how does it differ from conventional additive manufacturing of implants?**

**Model Answer:**
Bioprinting is an AM process that uses **living cells, biomaterials, and bio-inks** (e.g., alginate, GelMA, collagen) to fabricate 3D tissue-like structures, layer by layer. It differs from conventional implant AM in three ways:

1. **Material:** Bio-inks contain living cells, unlike metals or polymers used in regular AM.
2. **Process requirements:** Requires sterile environment, controlled temperature (**37 °C**), and humid incubation.
3. **Objective:** To create **functional living tissue** (skin, cartilage, bone) rather than inert implants.

Common bioprinting techniques include **extrusion-based, inkjet, and laser-assisted bioprinting**, each suited to specific cell viscosities and resolutions.

**Q2. List any four biocompatible materials used in additive manufacturing for medical implants.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5 or Grade 23 ELI):** Most widely used for orthopedic and spinal implants; excellent biocompatibility and osseointegration.
2. **CoCrMo Alloy:** Used in dental and joint replacements; high wear resistance.
3. **PEEK (Polyetheretherketone):** High-performance polymer for spinal cages; modulus (~3–4 GPa) closer to bone than metals.
4. **Hydroxyapatite (HA) / Tricalcium Phosphate (TCP):** Bio-ceramics for bone scaffolds; promote natural bone regeneration.

> [!NOTE]
> Additional valid answers: PMMA, Nylon-12 (PA2200), zirconia, tantalum.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023, ESE Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Discuss in detail the various medical applications of additive manufacturing. Cover implants, surgical guides, anatomical models, and tissue engineering, with examples and material specifications.**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Patient-Specific Implants and Prosthetics (7 marks)**

- **Orthopedic implants:** Custom acetabular cups (Lima Corporate's Delta TT, Stryker's Triathlon), spinal cages, cranial plates. Made via **DMLS of Ti-6Al-4V** with controlled porosity (**60–80 %**) for bone ingrowth.
- **Dental implants:** Custom abutments, crowns, bridges via **SLA / DLP** with resins like MED610 (USP Class VI biocompatible).
- **Lattice design rationale:** Using Gibson-Ashby model (Eq. 1), lattice modulus is tuned to match cortical bone (**10–30 GPa**), eliminating stress shielding.
- **Cranial reconstruction:** 3D-printed PEEK or titanium plates, designed from CT scans, allowing perfect fit in trauma cases.

**(b) Surgical Guides, Anatomical Models, and Tissue Engineering (7 marks)**

- **Surgical cutting guides:** FDM-printed in biocompatible polymers (e.g., MED610); guide the surgeon for accurate bone cuts during knee replacement.
- **Anatomical models:** Printed from CT/MRI data using **PolyJet (multi-material)** for realistic soft-tissue simulation; used in pre-operative planning and patient communication.
- **Tissue engineering scaffolds:** **Bioprinting** with hydrogels (alginate, GelMA) creates 3D scaffolds for cell seeding. Pore size (**200–500 µm**) optimized for cell migration and nutrient diffusion.
- **Hearing aids and prosthetics:** Custom-fit shells printed via SLA — production time reduced from weeks to a single day (e.g., Phonak, Oticon).
- **Future direction:** Vascularized organ printing and 4D bioprinting (shape-changing implants responsive to body temperature).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Explain the role of additive manufacturing in aerospace and automotive industries with specific case studies, materials, and weight reduction data.**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Aerospace Applications of AM (7 marks)**

- **Weight reduction through topology optimization:** Airbus A320 cabin bracket printed in **Scalmalloy (Al-Mg-Sc)** via EOS SLM, achieving **55 % weight reduction** with no loss in stiffness.
- **Part consolidation:** GE LEAP fuel nozzle — **18 parts consolidated into 1** via DMLS in CoCr alloy, **25 % lighter**, with 60 % improved fuel efficiency.
- **Complex internal features:** Conformal cooling channels in rocket engine injectors (SpaceX Raptor) impossible by conventional casting.
- **Material choices:** Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, Scalmalloy, ULTEM 9085 (FDM for cabin interiors).
- **On-demand spare parts:** Reduces inventory cost; Boeing partners with OEMs for digital warehousing of legacy aircraft spares.
- **Certification challenge:** Each part undergoes rigorous **CT scanning, NDT, and FEA validation** before flight clearance.

**(b) Automotive Applications of AM (7 marks)**

- **Functional prototypes and jigs:** FDM-printed jigs reduce lead time by **80 %** in mass-production lines.
- **End-use lightweight components:** SLM-printed **bionic brake caliper** by Bugatti and APWORKS (in Scalmalloy) — **40 % weight reduction**, equivalent stiffness.
- **Conformal cooling in injection molds:** DMLS inserts with internal cooling channels reduce cycle time by **40 %** and warpage in plastic parts.
- **Topology-optimized chassis components:** Porsche, BMW use SLM to print AlSi10Mg structural nodes.
- **Material range:** AlSi10Mg, Ti-6Al-4V, maraging steel 1.2709, carbon-fiber-reinforced nylon.
- **Future scope:** Customizable EV battery housings, range-extender brackets, and motorsport on-demand parts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** AM processes (DMLS vs SLM vs EBM) — examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if you mix them up.
> - **Always mention** the patient-specific value-chain (CT → STL → CAD → Print) in medical questions, not just "we use 3D printing."
> - **Always cite** the **Gibson-Ashby model** for any lattice-modulus question — this is the most-asked equation in KTU Module 4 papers.
> - **Avoid generic answers** like "AM reduces cost and time" — quantify with numbers (e.g., **"55 % weight reduction in Airbus bracket"**) to score full marks.
> - **Process-material pairing** is critical: SLA ≠ DMLS. Wrong pairing = **immediate 2-mark deduction**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Medical AM Workflow:** CT/MRI → DICOM → Segmentation → STL → CAD → Slicing → Print → Post-process → Implant.
- **Key Process-Material Pairings (must memorize):**
  * DMLS / SLM → **Ti-6Al-4V** (implants)
  * EBM → **Ti-6Al-4V** (large orthopedics)
  * SLA / DLP → **MED610 resin** (dental, hearing aids)
  * FDM → **PEEK** (spinal cages)
  * Bioprinting → **Alginate, GelMA** (scaffolds)
- **Gibson-Ashby Equation:** $E^* = C \cdot E_s \cdot (\rho^*/\rho_s)^2$ — defines lattice modulus scaling.
- **Strength Scaling:** $\sigma_{pl}^* = C_2 \cdot \sigma_{ys} \cdot (\rho^*/\rho_s)^{3/2}$ — plastic collapse of cellular solid.
- **Porosity (P):** $P = 1 - \rho^*/\rho_s$; optimal bone scaffold porosity = **60–80 %**.
- **Optimal Pore Size for Bone Ingrowth:** **200–500 µm**.
- **Modulus Matching (Cortical Bone):** **10–30 GPa** — solves stress shielding.
- **Standard Biocompatibility:** **ISO 10993**; **USP Class VI** for medical-grade resins.
- **Surface Roughness for Implants:** **Ra < 1 µm** (functional); **5–20 µm** (as-printed).
- **GE LEAP Fuel Nozzle:** 18 → 1 part consolidation, 25 % lighter, CoCr via DMLS.
- **Airbus A320 Bracket:** Scalmalloy (Al-Mg-Sc) via SLM, **55 % weight saving**.
- **Bugatti Bionic Brake Caliper:** SLM Scalmalloy, **40 % weight reduction**.
- **Conformal Cooling Inserts:** DMLS maraging steel, **40 % cycle time reduction** in injection molding.
- **Stress Shielding Problem:** Solid Ti implant E = 110 GPa ≫ bone (10–30 GPa); lattice reduces E to match.
- **Biocompatible Metals:** Ti-6Al-4V, CoCrMo, 316L SS, Tantalum, Niobium.
- **Biocompatible Polymers:** PEEK, PMMA, PLA, MED610, Nylon-12 (PA2200).
- **Bioceramics:** Hydroxyapatite (HA), β-Tricalcium Phosphate (β-TCP), Zirconia, Alumina.
- **ASTM Standards:** ASTM F2077 (spinal fatigue), ASTM F136 (Ti-6Al-4V), ASTM F75 (CoCr).
- **On-demand Spare Parts:** Digital warehousing in aerospace and defense reduces inventory cost.
- **4D Bioprinting:** Shape-responsive scaffolds that change geometry in response to body temperature/pH.
- **Voxel size trade-off:** Higher resolution → longer build time → higher cost. KTU exam may ask this trade-off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific — Frequently Tested Areas:**
> 1. Patient-specific implant workflow (always appears as 5–7 mark question)
> 2. Gibson-Ashby derivation (numerical expected every cycle)
> 3. Comparison table of medical AM processes
> 4. Aerospace case study (GE nozzle or Airbus bracket)
> 5. Stress shielding explanation with modulus numbers
> 6. Porosity and pore-size data for bone scaffolds
